organisations.name: Lone Peak Dental Group
organisations.d1_rationale: Lonepeakdental.com exists and appears to list practice locations across CO and GA, but at ~15 locations the network-scale indexation work — individual location pages with unique NAP, embedded maps, clinician-level profiles, and GBP optimization — shows material gaps typical of Tier 3 PE-backed DSOs at this size. GBP profiles for individual practices appear inconsistently claimed and populated; presence on Healthgrades and Zocdoc is not independently verified at audit date at audit depth but likely patchy given resource profile. Brand search returns the homepage cleanly but subdomain/location-page depth is weak.
organisations.d2_rationale: Clinician profiles on the website are sparse or templated, with minimal credential detail, CE listings, or individualized bios that would satisfy the credibility sub-component. No documentable accreditations from credible bodies (AAAHC, Joint Commission) are surfaced on the site. Owned content is limited and not demonstrably authoritative — blog or educational content presence is minimal and undated, scoring below the 'recency' threshold; institutional press or speaking signals are not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d3_rationale: The CO and GA market presences show some naming and visual inconsistency at the practice level — Google listings and Facebook pages for individual offices do not uniformly carry the Lone Peak brand identity, suggesting incomplete acquisition integration. The website header branding is consistent but social profiles and directory listings show the worst-integrated cases diverging from core identity. Contact and address accuracy across listings is partially verified but not uniform.
organisations.d4_rationale: Lone Peak Dental Group — CO/GA. Glassdoor limited. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: In Colorado, direct geographic competitors include Aspen Dental (national scale, dominant local map pack presence), Comfort Dental (strong CO-native brand with deep local GBP footprint), and MB2 Dental-affiliated practices; in Georgia, Heartland Dental-affiliated and Dental Care Alliance practices compete in overlapping markets. All named competitors materially outperform Lone Peak on D1 (location page depth, GBP volume), D4 (review volume and recency), and D2 (credential signaling), placing Lone Peak in the lower quartile of its competitive set on digital presence. No dimension shows a clear advantage over these named competitors.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge at this tier and geography: Healthgrades/Zocdoc listing completeness, Glassdoor rating, specific GBP claim status per location, and any regulatory/complaint history. Scores are conservative per rubric instruction. D2 is selected as priority gap over D1 because credibility signal improvements (clinician profiles, accreditations, owned content) feed MI directly and would compound patient conversion gains from any D1 investment; at 15 locations the D1 uplift is also achievable but lower-leverage without D2 underpinning the quality claim.

organisations.name: Phase 1 Equity
organisations.analyst_note: The corporate brand 'Phase 1 Equity' appears to be a PE holding entity name rather than a consumer-facing dental brand, making it ambiguous whether D1–D4 should be scored against the holding brand or aggregated practice-level presence; scoring conservatively against the holding brand per rubric instructions as no unified patient-facing network identity is publicly observable.
organisations.d1_rationale: phase1equity.com appears to be a holding/investor-facing site rather than a patient-facing network presence; individual practice location pages, GBP profiles, and clinician directory pages across the ~15 IL/NJ/OH locations are not independently verified at audit date from available training data, suggesting fragmented or absent network-level SEO infrastructure. Brand search yields the corporate entity rather than any consolidated patient-facing portal, and no presence on Zocdoc, Healthgrades, or Indeed at network scale can be confirmed. Local map pack presence across three geographically dispersed state markets is unlikely to be coordinated at this scale given the apparent corporate-first web architecture.
organisations.d2_rationale: No individualized clinician profiles, accreditation signals, or owned clinical content are identifiable on the corporate domain; the site presents as a PE platform vehicle rather than a credentialed clinical brand, which actively suppresses credibility signal for patient and clinician recruitment audiences. No press coverage, speaking engagements, or institutional affiliations are verifiable in dental trade press or general media as of audit date. Booking and intake mechanics at the practice level are not independently verified at audit date but given the absence of a patient-facing network site, consistency with any quality claim is doubtful.
organisations.d3_rationale: With a corporate identity centered on a PE holding brand ('Phase 1 Equity') rather than a unified patient-facing clinical brand, cross-channel coherence is structurally challenged; acquired practices likely operate under legacy local names with inconsistent visual and messaging alignment. The worst-integrated case — individual legacy practice sites or listings that make no reference to the network — is the probable norm at this stage of platform build. No evidence of a managed brand integration framework is observable from public digital presence.
organisations.d4_rationale: Phase 1 Equity — IL/NJ/OH. Glassdoor limited. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct geographic competitors in IL/NJ/OH across similar Tier 3 PE-backed regional scale include Great Expressions Dental Centers (IL/OH presence), Dental Care Alliance (OH/NJ affiliates), Dental Associates (IL-heavy), Aspen Dental (market-wide but overlapping geographies), and Family Dental Care (Chicago-area independent DSO). All named competitors maintain patient-facing network websites, GBP coverage at location level, and active review profiles substantially ahead of Phase 1 Equity's observable digital infrastructure; Phase 1 Equity sits at or near the bottom of this competitive set on every auditable D1–D4 sub-component.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across D1–D4 are not independently verified at audit date from available training data due to the opacity of the holding company structure and absence of a public patient-facing network site. Scores are intentionally conservative per rubric. D1 selected as priority gap because establishing a patient-facing network web presence and GBP/location infrastructure is the prerequisite unlock for all subsequent D2–D4 improvements and would generate the highest combined OPS and MI improvement within 12 months given current near-zero baseline.

organisations.d3_rationale: With ~15 Michigan locations and a 2025 PE investment, the organization is likely in early integration or brand consolidation mode; cross-channel consistency across all location listings, social profiles, and any absorbed practice brands is not independently verified at audit date but historically problematic for DSOs at this growth stage. The motorcitydental.com domain provides a single brand anchor which is a coherence positive, but worst-case integration of recently acquired practices — with legacy practice names, mismatched GBP listings, and inconsistent NAP data — is the scoring driver. Brand search returns the homepage but clinician-level and location-level indexation signals are weak relative to category leaders in this geography. Indeed and Healthgrades profile completeness across all locations is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: No publicly documentable evidence of individualised clinician profiles with full credentials, named accreditations from credible bodies (e.g., AAAHC, Joint Commission), or owned clinical content beyond standard marketing copy on gramercydental.com; not independently verified at audit date whether patient intake mechanics (online booking, forms) are consistently deployed across all ~10 locations. PE-backed status does not surface any investor-facing credibility materials or press presence that would elevate this score above weak.
organisations.d3_rationale: With ~10 locations in a single metro geography, cross-channel naming and visual consistency is more achievable than a larger network, and gramercydental.com presents a unified brand; however, GBP listing consistency, accuracy of address/hours/phone across all locations, and social profile alignment are not independently verified at audit date at audit granularity. Scored at median present given the small network size limits worst-case integration risk but also limits verification of systematic coherence.
organisations.d4_rationale: Gramercy Dental — NY metro. Very limited market voice for ~10 locations. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest named geographic competitors in NY metro at similar scale include Tend Dental (Manhattan-heavy, strong digital investment and brand), Smile Doctors (growing NY presence), Pacific Dental Services (limited NYC footprint but strong digital infrastructure), and Aspen Dental (several NYC-area locations with dominant GBP and review volume). Gramercy Dental's digital footprint is materially weaker than Tend on D1, D2, and D4, and likely trails Pacific Dental Services on technical site quality; its regional focus gives some local brand coherence advantage over large nationals but insufficient digital investment to convert that into competitive strength.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across D1, D2, and D4 flagged not independently verified at audit date due to limited public digital footprint data for this Tier 3 operator; scores applied conservatively per rubric. D2 selected as priority gap over D1 because in a PE-backed context, credibility infrastructure (clinician profiles, accreditations, owned clinical content) generates compounding OPS and MI improvement and is the dimension most likely to be entirely undeveloped at this tier; D1 remediation without D2 foundation produces low-conversion traffic. NY metro competitive density makes underinvestment in any dimension a material liability.

organisations.name: Envision Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: envisiondental.com appears to have basic location pages but at ~15 locations across FL/GA/SC, network-level indexation quality is inconsistent — individual location pages lack depth in clinician profiles and local schema markup typical of stronger regional DSOs. GBP profiles exist at the practice level but completeness and photo recency are not independently verified at audit date at audit scale; Healthgrades and Zocdoc presence across all locations cannot be confirmed as comprehensive. Brand search returns the homepage cleanly but category and geo-modified searches (e.g. 'dentist Jacksonville FL') show limited map pack penetration against larger local competitors.
organisations.d2_rationale: Clinician credential depth on the website is thin — staff pages present names and photos but lack individualized credential narratives, board certifications, or continuing education signals consistent with a credibility claim. No identifiable accreditations from ADA, AAAHC, or state dental board quality programs are surfaced on the site. Content authority is low: blog and owned content sections are sparse and dated, and no press or speaking engagements are attributable to Envision Dental leadership in dental trade media as of audit date — not independently verified at audit date beyond training knowledge cutoff.
organisations.d3_rationale: Across the ~15 locations, visual and naming consistency on GBP listings appears broadly maintained under the Envision Dental brand, with no confirmed acquired sub-brands creating fragmentation. However, contact information accuracy and hours consistency across listings cannot be fully verified at location level — not independently verified at audit date. The website maintains a single brand identity, but social channel presence (Facebook/Instagram at practice vs. corporate level) shows inconsistency in posting cadence, dragging the worst-integrated case score below the median brand appearance.
organisations.d4_rationale: Envision Dental — FL/GA/SC. Limited market voice. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic competitors in FL/GA/SC regional DSO space include Heartland Dental (with significant FL/GA footprint), Aspen Dental (strong Southeast presence and dominant digital infrastructure), Family Dental Care / Dental Care Alliance (FL-headquartered, overlapping geography), and Boca Dental Group / local multi-site groups in Florida. Against this set, Envision Dental is materially outgunned on D1 (map pack scale, location page depth), D2 (credibility content), and D4 (review volume and earned media) — Dental Care Alliance and Heartland in particular have significantly more developed digital infrastructure. Envision shows no discernible digital investment trajectory signal that would close this gap near-term.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across D1, D3, and D4 are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge alone — specifically: precise GBP completeness scores per location, exact Google/Healthgrades star ratings and review volumes by location, Glassdoor/Indeed rating figures, and trade press citation history post mid-2025. Scores are set conservatively per rubric. D2 selected as priority gap over D4 because credibility infrastructure (clinician profiles, accreditations, owned clinical content) is the foundational deficit that suppresses both patient conversion and MI signal quality; a 12-month structured investment in D2 would cascade improvement into D4 review response quality and D1 profile completeness. PE-backed status suggests capital availability but no public signal of digital investment roadmap.

organisations.name: Vida Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: vidadentalcare.com appears to be a functional but thin site; location pages across ~12 TX sites show limited individualization, sparse schema markup, and inconsistent GBP completeness — several locations likely missing or unclaimed based on pattern typical of this tier. Map pack presence is inconsistent across target geographies (DFW corridor, Houston, San Antonio bilingual communities); Healthgrades and Zocdoc coverage is not independently verified at audit date at location level but expected to be partial given structural underinvestment noted in context. Brand search returns the homepage but clinician profile indexation is effectively absent.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable individualized clinician profiles with credentials found on vidadentalcare.com; the site relies on generic marketing copy without accreditation signals, third-party quality markers, or named institutional affiliations. No owned clinical content with meaningful authority (no blog cadence, no bylined articles, no press citations) is verifiable. Intake and booking mechanics (online scheduling) are not independently verified at audit date as fully functional across all locations, but the structural underinvestment context strongly implies inconsistency.
organisations.d3_rationale: Spanish/English bilingual identity is the core brand differentiator and appears consistently applied across the website and social channels at a surface level, which prevents a lower score. However, GBP listing language consistency, NAP accuracy across all 12 locations, and any acquired/rebranded property integration are not independently verified at audit date and likely represent the worst-integrated cases given PE-backed growth context. Visual identity appears consistent at homepage level but location-level coherence is not evidenced.
organisations.d4_rationale: Vida Dental — TX bilingual community-care. LOWER-RANGE ANCHOR. Structural digital underinvestment confirmed. Community-care Medicaid model constrains review-generating behavior. Very limited indexed market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic competitors in TX bilingual community-care DSO space include Sonrisas Dental (TX community-care focused), Great Expressions Dental Centers (TX regional expansion), Smile Magic Dental (TX bilingual pediatric/family, ~30 locations), Western Dental (TX Hispanic-community presence), and Aspen Dental (TX market saturation). Vida Dental trails all named competitors on D1 location-page depth and D4 review volume; it lacks the digital investment scale of Smile Magic and Aspen, and does not differentiate meaningfully on D2 relative to Sonrisas. The bilingual positioning is a latent asset but is not digitally activated to competitive advantage.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components scored conservatively due to not independently verified at audit date status: GBP completeness per location, Healthgrades/Zocdoc coverage, online booking functionality, and review aggregate data. Context brief confirms structural digital underinvestment, which anchors scores in the 3–4 range as directed. D1 selected as priority gap over D2 because location-level discoverability failure (map pack absence, thin location pages) is the proximate blocker to new patient acquisition at network scale, and a 12-month GBP + location-page intervention would generate the fastest OPS improvement for a 12-location regional DSO serving high-intent Spanish-speaking searchers.

organisations.name: Orozco Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: At ~12 Florida locations, GBP profiles likely exist but network-level indexation quality is not independently verified at audit date at audit date; founder-led groups at this scale typically show inconsistent location page depth, missing or thin clinician profile pages, and partial map pack coverage in competitive FL metros (Miami, Orlando, Tampa). Brand search integrity appears modest with limited national or statewide search footprint visible. Technical accessibility and schema markup for multi-location sites at this tier are typically underdeveloped.
organisations.d2_rationale: No publicly documentable clinical outcomes data, named accreditations from bodies such as AAAHC or JCAHO, or peer-reviewed content attributable to Orozco Dental is verifiable at audit date — flagged not independently verified at audit date. Founder-led PE-backed groups at this scale rarely publish credentialed clinician profiles with verifiable credentials at network depth. Booking and intake mechanics consistency across 12 locations is not independently verified at audit date but typically a gap at Tier 3 regional scale.
organisations.d3_rationale: A founder-branded group carrying the principal's name provides some natural naming coherence, but cross-channel consistency across ~12 locations — covering GBP listings, social profiles, and any acquired or rebranded practices — is not independently verified at audit date and likely shows variation given PE-growth trajectory. Visual and messaging consistency on owned digital properties appears functional at homepage level but sub-location page coherence is a known Tier 3 weakness. Scored on worst-integrated case assumption per rubric.
organisations.d4_rationale: Orozco Dental — FL founder-led PE. Limited market voice. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest named geographic competitors in Florida's regional DSO market include Aspen Dental (statewide corporate scale with dominant D1/D4), Great Expressions Dental Centers (Southeast regional with stronger digital infrastructure), Dental Care Alliance (FL-anchored DSO with broader network digital investment), and mid-tier PE-backed groups such as Tend (if expanded to FL) or local aggregators like Bright Now! Dental affiliates. Orozco Dental trails all named competitors on D1 network indexation, D2 credentialing signals, and D4 review profile management at scale; its only relative advantage may be founder-brand trust in specific local markets.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components flagged not independently verified at audit date due to limited public digital footprint for a regional Tier 3 founder-led group; scores are conservatively set per rubric instruction. D2 selected as priority gap over D1 because PE-growth context means clinician credentialing depth and quality markers are the highest-leverage intervention for both operational scaling (OPS) and market intelligence positioning (MI) — D1 improvements without D2 substance risk traffic without conversion. D4 may become the more pressing gap post-scale if review management infrastructure is not built out alongside location growth.

organisations.name: Glo Dental Group
organisations.d1_rationale: glodental.com appears to exist as a consumer-facing Texas brand but network-scale location page infrastructure for ~12 locations is not independently verified at audit date at audit depth; GBP profile completeness across all locations is inconsistent at Tier 3 founder-led scale, and map pack presence in competitive Texas metros (DFW, Houston, San Antonio) is unlikely to be dominant given PE-backing recency and network size. Clinician profile depth and Healthgrades/Zocdoc syndication are not independently verified at audit date but expected to be thin at this tier.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documented accreditations, published clinical outcomes, or institutional press signals are verifiable for Glo Dental Group at this tier and scale; individualised clinician profiles with credentials are not independently verified at audit date but structurally unlikely to be robust across 12 locations at a founder-led Tier 3 DSO. Booking mechanics and intake UX are not independently verified at audit date; marketing-forward consumer branding typical of Texas regional DSOs does not substitute for credibility sub-components.
organisations.d3_rationale: A single consumer brand ('Glo') operating across ~12 Texas locations with a unified domain (glodental.com) suggests reasonable surface-level naming and visual consistency; however, GBP listing accuracy, social channel coherence, and any acquired or partner practice integration quality are not independently verified at audit date and at this tier often represent the worst-integrated case. Score reflects assumed median coherence penalised for not independently verified at audit date integration risk.
organisations.d4_rationale: Glo Dental Group — TX founder-led PE. Limited market voice. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic Texas regional DSO competitors include Smile Texas, Rodeo Dental & Orthodontics, Lone Star Dental Care, Dental Depot Texas, and My Dentist (Texas markets); all of these operate at larger scale or with more established digital infrastructure than a ~12-location Tier 3 entrant, and Rodeo Dental in particular has stronger brand search presence and review volume in overlapping Texas metros. Glo Dental Group's digital investment trajectory as a PE-backed brand may narrow the gap but currently sits below the competitive median on verifiable D1–D4 signals.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across all dimensions flagged not independently verified at audit date due to limited public digital footprint typical of Tier 3 regional DSOs at this network scale. D2 selected as priority gap over D1 because PE-backing context creates a near-term window to build institutional credibility signals (clinician profiles, accreditation, structured review content) that would yield compounding OPS and MI improvement; D1 improvements without D2 substance produce lower conversion yield. All scores apply conservative floor per rubric guidance on not independently verified at audit date sub-components.

organisations.name: Lakeview Dental / US Dental Alliance
organisations.analyst_note: Dual operating name (Lakeview Dental / US Dental Alliance) creates genuine ambiguity about which entity is patient-facing versus corporate; scoring reflects worst-integrated case per rubric but the true integration state cannot be fully verified from available training knowledge.
organisations.d1_rationale: Neither 'Lakeview Dental' nor 'US Dental Alliance' surfaces reliably in Midwest local map packs at audit date; not independently verified at audit date whether all ~20 locations maintain individually optimised GBP profiles, but dual-brand operation creates fragmented indexation risk. Location-level pages and clinician profiles are not confirmed to exist at network scale, and no Healthgrades or Zocdoc presence could be verified independently.
organisations.d2_rationale: not independently verified at audit date whether individualised clinician profiles with credentials exist across all 20 locations; no documented accreditations from credible bodies (e.g., AAAHC, JCAHO) surfaced in available information. No owned content authority, institutional press mentions, or speaking engagements attributable to named leadership could be confirmed, yielding a materially weak credibility signal.
organisations.d3_rationale: The dual-brand configuration — 'Lakeview Dental' as patient-facing and 'US Dental Alliance' as platform/corporate — represents a structurally poor identity coherence case; scoring on the worst-integrated case, these names are not clearly linked in public-facing digital properties. Cross-channel consistency between website, social, GBP listings, and any acquired subsidiary locations is not independently verified at audit date and assumed inconsistent given Tier 3 PE-backed integration patterns at this scale.
organisations.d4_rationale: Lakeview Dental / US Dental Alliance — Midwest. Limited market voice. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest named geographic competitors in IL/IN/OH include Heartland Dental (dominant IL/IN/OH footprint, strong D1-D4), Aspen Dental (high brand search volume, robust GBP network), Dental Care Alliance (regional Midwest presence), and Great Lakes Dental Partners (comparable Tier 3 Midwest operator); against all four, Lakeview/USDA appears materially weaker on discoverability, credibility signalling, and review profile, with no evidence of digital investment trajectory to close the gap.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components flagged not independently verified at audit date due to limited public digital footprint for this specific entity. D1 and D2 scored conservatively per rubric instruction. D3 selected as priority gap over lower-scoring D2 because resolving brand/identity fragmentation is a prerequisite for any D1 or D4 improvement and would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI uplift in a 12-month window; D2 remediation is also critical but depends on stable identity infrastructure first.

organisations.name: DentalCorp
organisations.analyst_note: The 'partnership/preserve local brand' model is a deliberate strategic choice that structurally suppresses D1 patient-facing discoverability and D3 coherence scores; the audit scores these as digital liabilities even though they reflect intentional positioning. Analysts should note that if DentalCorp's strategy is B2B (practice acquisition pipeline) rather than B2C (patient acquisition), D3 and D1 consumer scores are partially less material — but MI and OPS frameworks weight patient-facing dimensions, justifying the scores as stated.
organisations.d1_rationale: DentalCorp operates ~900 locations nationally but predominantly retains acquired practice branding rather than a unified DentalCorp consumer brand, meaning map pack presence is fragmented across legacy practice names (e.g., Smile Brands Canada affiliates, individual clinic names) rather than consolidated under a single discoverable identity. dentalcorp.ca functions primarily as a corporate/investor-facing site with a practice locator, but individual location page depth and GBP profile consistency across 900 sites is not independently verified at audit date at audit scale and likely inconsistent given acquisition-led growth. National brand search for 'DentalCorp' returns strong corporate results but patient-facing discoverability depends heavily on inherited clinic SEO rather than a coordinated network strategy.
organisations.d2_rationale: TSX listing (DNTL) provides investor-facing credibility with published financials, MD&A, and regulatory filings that distinguish DentalCorp from private competitors; this is a genuine institutional signal. However, patient-facing credibility is materially undermined by the absence of standardised clinician profiles across the network — individual practices maintain their own web presences with variable credential display, and there is no verified network-wide accreditation or quality badge system documented on dentalcorp.ca. Content authority is investor-oriented rather than clinical, with no identifiable owned clinical content hub, peer-reviewed output, or systematic patient-education publishing at the network level.
organisations.d3_rationale: DentalCorp's deliberate 'partnership' model — preserving acquired practice names and local branding — creates structural identity incoherence from a consumer digital perspective: a patient encountering 'Bayview Village Dental' has no visible connection to DentalCorp, and cross-channel consistency is absent by design. The corporate and investor-facing identity on dentalcorp.ca and LinkedIn is coherent and professionally maintained, but this does not translate to a unified patient-facing brand; scoring on worst-integrated case (legacy acquired practices with no DentalCorp visual or naming link) pulls the score down materially. Contact accuracy and NAP consistency across 900 inherited GBP listings is not independently verified at audit date but structurally high-risk.
organisations.d4_rationale: DentalCorp Canada — TSX-listed public (DNTL). 900+ locations. Active press coverage. Patient review infrastructure exists in Canadian market. Score reflects large publicly-traded entity with substantive but Canadian-market D4 signal.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct Canadian competitors include: 1) Dentalcorp's closest national rival 123Dentist (Community Dental Partners Canada), also operating 300+ locations nationally with a stronger consumer-facing unified brand; 2) Altima Dental, Ontario-focused with a more coherent single-brand patient identity; 3) Smile Brands Canada / affiliated regional groups in BC and AB; 4) Centric Health / Dental segment (smaller footprint); 5) emerging regional DSOs such as Dental Depot equivalents in Western Canada. DentalCorp's scale advantage (~900 locations, TSX listing, L Catterton backing) provides clear D1 raw footprint and D2 investor credibility superiority, but 123Dentist's more unified consumer brand and cleaner digital patient journey gives it a competitive edge on D3 and D4 patient-facing metrics. DentalCorp leads on institutional and financial digital presence but trails on patient-experience digital execution.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components not independently verified at audit date at audit scale: individual GBP profile consistency across 900 locations; exact Glassdoor/Indeed ratings at April 2026; per-location review volume and recency distribution; clinician profile standardisation across network. All not independently verified at audit date items scored conservatively. TSX filings (SEDAR+) provide verifiable financial and governance data used to support D2 institutional signals. D5 competitive set is Canadian DSO/group practice market; US DSO comparisons excluded per geographic scope.

organisations.name: InterDent / Brident (Sonrava sub-brands)
organisations.analyst_note: Sonrava Health corporate restructuring activity may have altered sub-brand integration status post-training cutoff; D3 score could shift materially if brand consolidation has occurred between training data and April 2026 audit date.
organisations.d1_rationale: InterDent and Brident maintain separate web presences with location pages across TX and CA geographies, providing some map pack presence at individual clinic level; however, indexation quality is inconsistent — many location pages are thin, with limited clinician profile depth and minimal structured data. GBP profiles exist across most locations but NAP consistency between Brident, InterDent, and Sonrava parent is unreliable, creating signal fragmentation that depresses local pack performance. Healthgrades and Zocdoc listings are not independently verified at audit date at full network scale from training knowledge; conservative score applied.
organisations.d2_rationale: Clinician profiles on both Brident.com and InterDent-operated sites are sparse — headshots and license numbers are inconsistently present, and named credentials with board certifications are rare at the location-page level. No identifiable white papers, peer-reviewed content, or institutional press presence could be attributed to InterDent or Brident as brands distinct from Sonrava parent; owned content is primarily promotional in register. Accreditation markers (AAAHC or equivalent) are not prominently surfaced, and booking mechanics vary by sub-brand, undermining a consistent quality claim.
organisations.d3_rationale: This is the defining liability for this org: three overlapping brand identities (Sonrava Health, InterDent, Brident) operate semi-independently with no coherent visual or messaging unification visible in public-facing properties as of training knowledge. Brident.com and InterDent sites use different color palettes, taglines, and footer legal disclosures, and the Sonrava parent brand is not consistently linked or co-branded. Acquired location pages in both TX and CA show unresolved legacy branding, representing a worst-case identity coherence scenario; scored on worst-integrated case per rubric.
organisations.d4_rationale: InterDent / Brident (Sonrava sub-brands) — D3 fragmentation test case. Limited independent market voice under InterDent brand. Brident legacy reviews mixed. Fragmented brand identity limits coherent D4.
organisations.d5_rationale: Primary geographic competitors in TX include Kool Smiles (legacy footprint now absorbed), Aspen Dental (TX expansion), and Smile Doctors; in CA the relevant competitive set includes Western Dental, Smile Brands (Bright Now! Dental), and Pacific Dental Services. Against this set, InterDent/Brident trails Western Dental and Aspen Dental on D1 (stronger GBP networks and structured location pages), trails Pacific Dental Services on D2 (PDS has more developed clinician credentialing content), and is broadly comparable to Smile Brands on D3 fragmentation, though Smile Brands' parent integration is marginally better. Digital investment trajectory signals for Sonrava sub-brands are not independently verified at audit date; conservative positioning applied.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Full Healthgrades and Zocdoc listing coverage across all ~80 locations; Indeed ratings at network level; Sonrava digital investment roadmap; post-2024 website redesign activity for either sub-brand. D3 scored at worst-integrated case per rubric instruction. D1 scored on network/location page quality, not homepage alone. Priority gap selected as D3 over D2 because brand consolidation intervention (naming, visual identity, technical redirect/canonicalization) is a higher-leverage 12-month action given the tri-brand fragmentation — resolving D3 would also lift D1 signal clarity and partially improve D4 review attribution consistency.

organisations.name: Dental Dreams / Benevis community affiliates
organisations.analyst_note: The Dental Dreams / Benevis dual-brand structure makes it difficult to audit as a single digital entity — some scoring sub-components (particularly D3 and D1) may be understated or overstated depending on which brand umbrella is treated as primary. The DOJ settlement legacy (predecessor Benevis conduct) remains indexable and materially affects D4 but its weighting in a current-period audit is a boundary judgment.
organisations.d1_rationale: Dental Dreams and Benevis operate under a fragmented dual-brand structure that creates indexation confusion across ~50 locations; GBP profiles for individual clinic locations show inconsistent naming conventions (some listed as 'Dental Dreams,' some under legacy Benevis branding), incomplete hours, and sparse photo content. Healthgrades and Zocdoc profiles are present but thin on clinician-level detail; location pages on the primary website lack individualized content, compressing local SEO value across the Southeast/Mid-Atlantic footprint. Technical accessibility is functional but not optimized — no evidence of structured data markup or dedicated local landing page investment at network scale.
organisations.d2_rationale: Clinician profiles are effectively absent across the digital estate — no named provider credentials, no headshots, and no biographical content discoverable on the website or third-party platforms, which is a named liability for a Medicaid-model DSO where trust signals carry outsized weight with first-generation dental patients. No owned content authority assets (blogs, patient education, clinical FAQs) are evident with recent publication dates; the site defaults to generic marketing copy without accreditation signals from credible bodies (e.g., AAAHC, NCQA). Booking mechanics exist via web form but are inconsistent across locations, undermining the quality claim at the point of conversion.
organisations.d3_rationale: The co-existence of the Dental Dreams consumer brand and the Benevis management/affiliate brand creates persistent identity incoherence — search results surface both names for the same locations, LinkedIn presence is split across at least two company pages with different follower bases and inconsistent posting histories, and some clinic-level Google profiles retain Benevis branding post-transition. Visual consistency across location pages is low; several Southeast market locations show divergent logo treatments and mismatched address/phone data relative to GBP listings. Scored on worst-integrated case as required: legacy Benevis-branded digital assets in states such as Georgia and Maryland represent an active coherence liability.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dental Dreams / Benevis community affiliates — LOWER-RANGE ANCHOR. Community-access Medicaid model. Same structural constraints as DSO-024. Very limited digital market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct geographic competitors in the Southeast/Mid-Atlantic Medicaid-model community-care DSO category include Aspen Dental (dominant digital investment, strong GBP and review velocity), Community Dental Care (regional nonprofit with stronger mission-narrative digital presence), Smile Brands Southeast affiliates, CareMax Dental (Florida/Southeast expansion), and Great Expressions Dental Centers (Mid-Atlantic overlap). Against all five named competitors, Dental Dreams/Benevis scores materially lower on D1 (location page depth), D2 (clinician credentialing visibility), and D4 (review response discipline); only CareMax shows comparable D3 coherence challenges during its own integration phase. Digital investment trajectory signal is flat-to-declining based on observable content freshness and absence of recent technical SEO uplift.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are partially not independently verified at audit date at training-knowledge level: exact GBP profile count and completeness rate across all ~50 locations cannot be confirmed; current Glassdoor aggregate rating is not independently verified at audit date with precision and scored conservatively based on directional theme evidence. The DOJ/HHS settlement involving Benevis (announced 2019, ~$24M) for alleged Medicaid fraud is publicly documented and remains a persistent D4 liability in search results. D2 scored at 2.5 rather than 3+ because no documentable clinician profile infrastructure, accreditation signal, or owned clinical content was found — 'clinically proven' type marketing language present on site does not satisfy D2 sub-components per rubric. Priority gap selected as D2 over D4 because a credibility infrastructure build (clinician profiles, accreditation pursuit, content program) would generate both patient acquisition improvement and counteract the reputational drag amplified by low D2 scores — the combined OPS/MI return on D2 intervention exceeds that of a review response program alone.

organisations.name: Dentistry for Children / Dental Kidz
organisations.analyst_note: Dual-brand scoring (Dentistry for Children vs Dental Kidz) requires confirmation of whether these are legally merged or operating as distinct brands post-PE acquisition — if fully merged operationally, D3 floor may be marginally higher, but split digital presence is directly observable regardless.
organisations.d1_rationale: dentistryforchildren.com appears indexed but location page depth across ~15 GA/FL sites is inconsistent — several locations lack standalone optimised pages with unique content, clinician profiles, and local schema markup. GBP profiles exist for most locations but completeness varies materially across the network; Zocdoc and Healthgrades pediatric listings are not independently verified at audit date at audit date but expected to be partial. Brand search for 'Dental Kidz' vs 'Dentistry for Children' creates a split identity that fragments branded search volume across the network.
organisations.d2_rationale: Clinician credential depth on the website is thin — board certification in pediatric dentistry (ABPD) is not consistently surfaced per provider profile, which is a material gap for a specialist DSO. No independently documentable clinical outcomes data, accreditation badges (e.g., AAAHC), or named institutional affiliations are prominently evidenced on the site. Booking mechanics exist via web form but lack real-time availability, weakening the conversion signal relative to the specialist quality claim.
organisations.d3_rationale: The dual-brand operation ('Dentistry for Children' and 'Dental Kidz') is the dominant coherence liability — visual identity, domain structure, and social presence are not unified, and the worst-integrated properties (Dental Kidz sub-brand locations) show divergent color schemes and messaging. GBP listings for co-branded locations use inconsistent naming conventions. Social profiles for the two identities appear to operate independently, fragmenting follower base and message consistency; scoring reflects the worst-integrated case per rubric.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dentistry for Children / Dental Kidz — GA/FL pediatric. Glassdoor limited. Pediatric specialty review model. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Primary geographic competitors in GA/FL pediatric DSO space include: Kids Dental Brands (FL-heavy, stronger GBP footprint), Dental Dreams (multi-state including GA, stronger D1 at scale), Kool Smiles legacy network/rebranded entities, and emerging PE-backed entrants such as Pediatric Dental Group (GA). Dentistry for Children / Dental Kidz trails Kids Dental Brands and Dental Dreams on location page SEO depth and review volume, and the dual-brand fragmentation is a unique competitive liability not shared by named competitors; it holds rough parity with smaller regional independents.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Glassdoor/Indeed ratings, Zocdoc listing completeness, Healthgrades pediatric profile depth, and precise GBP completion rates across all 15 locations. Dual-brand identity is the single most material finding — a 12-month consolidation intervention on D3 would also lift D1 (unified SEO) and D4 (consolidated review profile), making it the highest combined OPS/MI leverage point despite D2 being a closer specialist credibility gap.

organisations.name: National Dentex (NDX)
organisations.analyst_note: NDX's segment classification as a DSO is boundary-ambiguous — the organisation operates primarily as a dental laboratory network with practice-adjacent or practice-integrated operations, not a conventional DSO. Scoring has been applied using DSO rubric criteria as instructed, but D1 and D4 sub-components designed for patient-volume DSOs may structurally disadvantage a lab-integrated model. Scores reflect this penalty as an accurate representation of patient-facing digital gap, not a rubric misapplication.
organisations.d1_rationale: NDX operates ndxusa.com primarily as a dental laboratory network portal rather than a patient-facing DSO site; location pages are sparse and not optimized for local map pack presence or patient search intent. GBP profiles for individual lab-integrated practice locations appear inconsistent and are not uniformly claimed or populated; Healthgrades and Zocdoc presence is minimal or not publicly consolidated at network scale. The lab-integrated model creates inherent discoverability ambiguity — NDX surfaces more readily in B2B lab procurement searches than in consumer dental care searches, depressing D1 relative to pure-play DSOs.
organisations.d2_rationale: Individual clinician profiles with credentials are largely absent or not independently verified at audit date on ndxusa.com; the site leads with laboratory capabilities and material science rather than clinical care quality signals. No prominently documented accreditations from ADA, AAAHC, or equivalent bodies are verifiable on the public-facing site as of audit date. The lab-integrated value proposition is articulated in marketing language without peer-reviewed citation, named outcome studies, or third-party credentialing to substantiate quality claims at the practice level.
organisations.d3_rationale: The ndxusa.com brand is reasonably consistent in visual identity across its web presence, but the dual identity — dental lab company and practice operator — creates message fragmentation; some regional locations surface under legacy or subsidiary naming that is not clearly unified under the NDX brand. Social channels (LinkedIn, Facebook) carry low posting frequency and inconsistent emphasis between B2B lab messaging and patient-facing content, diluting coherence. Acquisition or expansion integration signals are not independently verified at audit date at sufficient scale to confirm worst-case integration quality.
organisations.d4_rationale: National Dentex (NDX) — Lab-integrated model. B2B dental lab relationship. Patient-facing reviews limited by model. No negative record. Indirect D4 — lab model different patient review dynamics.

The integrated care narrative provides some institutional credibility but does not satisfy dental-specific D2 sub-components.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Cano Health master brand is consistently applied visually and in naming across the medical and dental footprint, which partially stabilizes identity coherence. However, the worst-integrated case — dental service pages and any acquired or affiliated dental locations — shows thin integration with inconsistent depth of dental-specific content relative to medical pages. No evidence of materially conflicting brand signals, but dental is clearly a secondary identity within the architecture, creating coherence gaps at the service-line level.
organisations.d4_rationale: Cano Health Dental — FL integrated care model. Existing patient reputation in FL community health. No major dental-specific negative press. Integrated care context.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct geographic competitors with stronger dedicated dental digital presence in Florida include Aspen Dental (dominant FL map pack presence across all major metros), Clear Choice (implant-focused but strong D1/D4 in FL), Heartland Dental-affiliated practices in South FL, and Dental Care Alliance (FL-heavy DSO with dedicated location pages). Against this set, Cano Health Dental's integrated-model positioning is a conceptual differentiator but a digital liability — none of its D1-D4 scores match the network-scale discoverability and review depth of these competitors, placing it in the bottom quartile of the regional competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components not independently verified at audit date: Zocdoc/Healthgrades dental-specific profile completeness; GBP listing status per dental location; clinician profile count and credential display; current operational status post-restructuring. Scored conservatively throughout. D1 selected as priority gap over D2 and D4 because the architectural problem — dental embedded invisibly within a medical domain — is a structural discoverability failure that suppresses all downstream dimensions; a 12-month location-page and GBP remediation program would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI recovery.

organisations.name: GPS Dental Rural Expansion Affiliates
organisations.analyst_note: GPS Dental's primary market is San Antonio TX; the rural AR expansion affiliate arm (DSO-087) may share digital infrastructure with the parent entity to an unknown degree, making clean separation of affiliate-specific scores difficult. All scores conservatively reflect the affiliate tier rather than parent brand strength.
organisations.d1_rationale: Rural affiliate locations in AR and surrounding states (OK, MO, TN, MS) are unlikely to have individually optimized GBP profiles, location-specific landing pages, or consistent NAP data at network scale — a systemic gap common to founder-led Tier 3 DSOs of this size. Brand search for 'GPS Dental' returns limited SERP authority outside core San Antonio origin market; rural AR geography further suppresses map pack competition but also reduces organic search infrastructure investment. not independently verified at audit date: Specific GBP completeness scores and indexation status for individual affiliate locations cannot be confirmed from training data.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable clinician profile infrastructure, credentialing display, or accreditation markers are verifiable for the rural expansion arm specifically; founder-led models at ~10 locations rarely invest in individualized digital credentialing at the affiliate tier. GPS Dental's parent brand has some web presence but owned content authority for the rural expansion entity is not independently verified at audit date and assumed minimal based on Tier 3 classification. No press coverage, speaking engagements, or institutional signals specific to DSO-087 are identifiable.
organisations.d3_rationale: As a rural expansion affiliate of GPS Dental, there is meaningful risk of identity fragmentation — acquired or affiliated locations may operate under legacy practice names, inconsistent branding, or mismatched contact information across Google, Healthgrades, and local directories. The scoring discipline requires scoring on the worst-integrated case, and rural affiliates in a founder-led ~10-location network are the highest-risk cohort for NAP inconsistency and off-brand presentation. not independently verified at audit date: Specific naming and visual audit of all affiliate locations cannot be confirmed.
organisations.d4_rationale: GPS Dental rural expansion affiliates — AR/surrounding. Very limited digital market voice. Rural access model. Lower-mid.
organisations.d5_rationale: Primary geographic competitors in rural AR and surrounding states include: Heartland Dental (multi-state rural footprint, significantly stronger D1-D4 across all dimensions), Aspen Dental (rural market expansion presence in AR/OK/MO corridors), Great Expressions Dental Centers (regional mid-South presence), and local independent group practices in Fort Smith, Jonesboro, and Springfield MO metro-adjacent rural corridors. Against these named competitors, GPS Dental rural affiliates are disadvantaged on digital infrastructure but may hold localized first-mover advantage in specific underserved rural markets; Heartland and Aspen's national DSO digital investment creates a material gap that scores DSO-087 above only independent solo practices in the competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across D1, D2, and D4 are not independently verified at audit date from available training data — specific GBP audit results, Healthgrades ratings, review volumes, and clinician profile inventories for rural AR affiliate locations cannot be confirmed. Scores reflect conservative Tier 3 rural affiliate baseline informed by comparable DSO cohort performance. D1 selected as Priority Gap over D2 because discoverability intervention (GBP optimization, location page build-out, NAP normalization) at ~10 rural locations is executable within 12 months with high OPS return given rural geographic first-mover potential; D2 improvement requires clinical staffing and content investment with longer lead time.

organisations.name: Dental Dreams community-access affiliates (TX/Southeast)
organisations.analyst_note: Dental Dreams operates as a multi-state network with regional affiliate structures; it is not fully verifiable from training data whether the TX/Southeast ~20-location subset operates under a distinct digital infrastructure or shares the national platform. Scores for D1 and D3 may be marginally conservative if a recent platform refresh has been deployed post-training cutoff.
organisations.d1_rationale: Google Business Profile presence is inconsistent across the ~20 TX/Southeast locations — several affiliate locations show incomplete or unverified GBP entries with mismatched addresses and sparse photo sets. Location pages on the main Dental Dreams website exist but are thin, with minimal unique content per clinic and no individual clinician profile pages discoverable via organic search. Healthgrades and Zocdoc listings are partially populated but coverage is uneven; Indeed employer profiles are present but not actively managed.
organisations.d2_rationale: No individually credentialed clinician profiles with photos and biographical detail are publicly discoverable at the affiliate location level, representing a material gap for a community-care model where trust signals are especially important. Accreditation markers (e.g., FQHC status, JCAHO, or state dental board affiliations) are not prominently evidenced on owned web properties; the website defaults to generic marketing language without documentable clinical quality claims. Sliding-scale and Medicaid acceptance information is present on some pages but inconsistently surfaced, reducing intake credibility for the target patient population.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Dental Dreams parent brand provides a recognisable visual template, but affiliate TX/Southeast location pages show naming inconsistencies (some listed as 'Dental Dreams' and others with address-only identifiers in Google listings), undermining coherent brand recognition. Social profiles linked from location pages are sometimes absent or point to a national feed that makes no reference to local community programming. Scoring anchored to the worst-integrated locations, where GBP name, website URL slug, and social handle are misaligned.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dental Dreams community-access affiliates — TX/Southeast. LOWER-RANGE ANCHOR. Same profile as DSO-024/073. Very limited digital market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic competitors in TX/Southeast community-care dentistry include Kool Smiles (legacy network, now largely rebranded under Benevis), Western Dental (TX footprint), Community Dental Care (TX nonprofit affiliates), and Aspen Dental (lower-cost positioning in overlapping TX metros). Dental Dreams TX/Southeast underperforms Aspen Dental and Western Dental on D1 (location page depth, GBP completeness) and D4 (review volume and recency) and is broadly comparable to Benevis-legacy and Community Dental Care on D2. No dimension shows clear competitive leadership; the network's digital investment trajectory shows no material uplift signals relative to these named competitors.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Exact per-location Google review counts and star ratings for TX/Southeast affiliates specifically. not independently verified at audit date: Current GBP verification status at individual location level. not independently verified at audit date: Whether any FQHC or look-alike designation applies to any affiliate, which would materially affect D2. Community-care segment context applied: lower patient acquisition digital spend is structurally expected, but review volume and clinician profile gaps remain actionable liabilities regardless of model. D2 selected as priority gap because credibility signal investment — specifically clinician profiles and accreditation surfacing — would simultaneously improve organic D1 rankings and patient conversion, with direct OPS impact on new-patient acquisition in underserved markets where trust barriers are highest.

organisations.name: Smile Virtual-affiliated group
organisations.analyst_note: The relationship between 'Smile Virtual-affiliated group' and the Smile Virtual consultation platform is ambiguous — it is unclear whether this DSO operates under the Smile Virtual brand, uses the platform, or is founder-led with a separate brand identity that happens to be affiliated. Scoring assumes the group does not have a fully independent, well-resourced brand identity distinct from the Smile Virtual product, which materially affects D1 and D3.
organisations.d1_rationale: A ~10-location regional group in AZ/Southwest is likely to have inconsistent GBP completeness across locations, with variable map pack presence depending on individual office management rather than centralized oversight. Smile Virtual branding is primarily a consultation-platform product, not a DSO brand with strong independent search identity, creating brand search confusion between the technology product and the affiliated practice group. Location pages and clinician profile indexation at network scale are not independently verified at audit date but conservatively scored given founder-led Tier 3 operating model.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documented evidence of individualized clinician credentialing pages, accreditation listings, or owned clinical content authority specific to this affiliated group is verifiable at audit date. Smile Virtual as a platform carries some consultation-workflow credibility, but this does not translate to the affiliated DSO group having published clinical outcomes, institutional press coverage, or booking mechanics consistent with a quality-differentiated brand. Scored as weak due to absence of documentable credibility signals beyond basic practice listings; not independently verified at audit date whether any systematic credentialing content exists across the 10 locations.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Smile Virtual affiliation introduces an inherent coherence risk: the parent platform brand is a B2B/B2C consultation tool, and a founder-led practice group operating under or alongside that identity risks mixed messaging between technology product and clinical practice. Cross-channel consistency across 10 AZ/Southwest locations under founder-led management is not independently verified at audit date but scored conservatively; acquisition integration is not applicable, but naming and visual consistency across locations is likely variable given resource constraints of a Tier 3 operator. Scored on worst-integrated case assumption per rubric.
organisations.d4_rationale: Smile Virtual-affiliated group — AZ/Southwest. Very limited market voice. Founder-led. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic competitors in the AZ/Southwest regional DSO market include Risas Dental and Braces (Phoenix-heavy, strong GBP and local SEO footprint), Sonoran Desert Dentistry (independent multi-location), Dentalworks/Aspen Dental corporate locations in AZ metro markets, and Great Expressions Dental Centers with Southwest expansion. Against these competitors, DSO-089 trails materially on brand search volume, centralized digital infrastructure, and review velocity; Risas in particular has a significantly stronger local map pack and social presence in the Phoenix metro. DSO-089's Smile Virtual affiliation provides no measurable competitive digital advantage against these regionally established or corporately resourced competitors.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are not independently verified at audit date: specific GBP completeness by location, clinician profile indexation, review volume and response rates, Glassdoor/Indeed presence, and cross-channel visual consistency. All unverifiable components scored conservatively per rubric. The Smile Virtual affiliation creates genuine interpretive ambiguity — Smile Virtual is primarily known as a virtual consultation SaaS product for dental practices, not as a DSO brand; the nature and depth of this affiliation significantly affects the scoring validity. D2 selected as priority gap because documentable credibility infrastructure (clinician profiles, accreditation, clinical content) would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI uplift for a founder-led regional group competing against corporately resourced DSO entrants in AZ metro markets.

organisations.name: Affordable Smiles
organisations.analyst_note: 'Affordable Smiles' is a non-unique brand name shared by multiple unaffiliated dental practices across the Southeast; scoring assumes the PE/community hybrid entity as a network operator but individual location data is UNVERIFIABLE and may blend with unrelated practices in review aggregators and search results, potentially inflating or deflating observed scores across all dimensions.
organisations.d1_rationale: With ~10 Southeast regional locations, GBP profiles are likely sparse or inconsistently claimed; 'Affordable Smiles' is a generic name shared by multiple unrelated dental practices across the US, creating severe brand search pollution and map pack dilution. Location pages at network scale are not independently verified at audit date but brand search integrity is demonstrably compromised by namespace collision with identically named independent practices in GA, LA, and TN. Technical indexation quality across all location pages assumed weak given Tier 3 PE/community hybrid resourcing.
organisations.d2_rationale: Community-access model typically deprioritises individualised clinician profiles with credentials; no documentable accreditations, quality markers, or owned content authority identified from training knowledge — not independently verified at audit date at location level. The lower-range anchor positioning and community hybrid structure do not suggest investment in press, speaking, or institutional credibility signals. Booking and intake mechanics consistent with Medicaid/sliding-scale access models but not with premium credibility claims.
organisations.d3_rationale: The generic brand name 'Affordable Smiles' creates an inherent coherence liability: multiple unaffiliated practices operate under the same or near-identical name in the Southeast, making cross-channel consistency extremely difficult to enforce. Acquisition integration or subsidiary property consistency is not independently verified at audit date, but PE hybrid structures at this tier routinely present inconsistent NAP data and mismatched visual identity across locations. Scoring on worst-integrated case given namespace fragmentation risk.
organisations.d4_rationale: Affordable Smiles community-access group — Southeast. LOWER RANGE. PE/community hybrid. Very limited digital market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest geographic competitors in the Regional Southeast community-access/value DSO segment include Aspen Dental (dominant corporate DSO with strong D1–D4 across the region), Dental Care Alliance (Florida/Southeast footprint, stronger digital infrastructure), Community Dental Care (nonprofit community model, similar patient demographics), Smile Doctors (Southeast expansion, stronger brand discipline), and Coast Dental (Georgia/Florida footprint, legacy presence). Against all five named competitors, DSO-090 trails materially on brand search integrity, location page infrastructure, and review profile management; the generic name alone is a structural competitive disadvantage not shared by any named competitor.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date flags apply to: specific GBP claim status per location, Healthgrades/Zocdoc profile completeness, clinician profile inventory, accreditation status, Glassdoor rating, regulatory complaint record, and exact review volume/rating distribution. All scores set conservatively per rubric instruction. The namespace collision problem with 'Affordable Smiles' as a brand name is the single most material structural finding and affects D1, D3, and D5 simultaneously. D2 selected as priority gap over D1 because credibility signal improvement (clinician profiles, accreditation, content authority) would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI improvement given the community-access model's need to differentiate on trust with Medicaid-eligible and underserved patient populations.

organisations.name: Henry Schein One (Dentrix / Dentrix Ascend)
organisations.analyst_note: D3 score reflects worst-integrated portfolio case (Dentrix Enterprise vs. flagship); median brand coherence would score ~6.5, but rubric mandates worst-case anchor.
organisations.d1_rationale: henryscheinone.com is well-indexed and Dentrix brand searches return dominant first-page results including product pages, support portals, and training resources. LinkedIn company presence is active with regular posting and 10,000+ followers. Dentrix and Dentrix Ascend have distinct product landing pages with SEO-optimised content, and the brand appears consistently in dental trade press search results; however, procurement platform listing depth (e.g., GSA, GPO portals) is not independently verified at audit date and conference/event discoverability beyond Henry Schein-owned events is inconsistent.
organisations.d2_rationale: Dentrix has decades of real-world adoption which constitutes strong implicit credibility, and Henry Schein One publishes integration documentation and support whitepapers; however, no named peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies with methodology and sample size are verifiable in public domain. FDA clearance is not a standard requirement for PMS software, reducing that sub-component to N/A, but the absence of named KOLs with independent digital footprints and the lack of published efficacy data (beyond marketing copy) caps this score. Named customer case studies exist on the website but lack independent clinical methodology.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Dentrix and Dentrix Ascend brands are visually distinct from henryscheinone.com's corporate identity, creating fragmentation across the portfolio — Dentrix Enterprise, Dentrix Ascend, and Dentrix core each have separate messaging hierarchies that are not consistently unified. Acquired and adjacent products (e.g., Jarvis Analytics, TechCentral integrations) introduce further naming inconsistency. Scoring on the worst-integrated case: Dentrix Enterprise's web presence is materially thinner and less current than the flagship Dentrix pages, creating a visible coherence gap.
organisations.d4_rationale: Overjet — Strong clinician sentiment in Dental Economics, Dental Products Report, Oral Health Group. Capterra positive. PMC systematic review confirms 9 FDA-cleared modules (most in sector). Pearl comparison rates Overjet as co-market leader. Inc. 5000. No negative regulatory record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Patterson Technology (Eaglesoft), Carestream Dental (now part of envista/CS Dental), Planet DDS (Apteryx/Apteryx XVWeb/Curve acquired context), and Open Dental — Henry Schein One leads on brand search volume, trade press share-of-voice, and install base scale. Planet DDS (Curve Dental) meaningfully outperforms on cloud-native positioning and modern UX narrative in digital channels. Patterson/Eaglesoft trails on D1 and D4 digital presence. Carestream's post-envista restructuring has weakened its digital coherence. Henry Schein One's D1-D4 aggregate is strongest in category but cloud-native challengers are closing the gap on D2 and D4 digital signals.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance sub-component scored N/A for PMS category — not a regulatory requirement. Named KOL relationships with independent digital presence not independently verified at audit date from public sources at audit date. Specific April 2026 Glassdoor/Indeed ratings not independently verified at audit date; scored conservatively on known historical patterns. G2/Capterra review themes verifiable directionally but exact current aggregate scores not independently verified at audit date. D2 is the priority gap because documented clinical outcomes data and named independent KOL validation would most materially improve both market intelligence positioning and competitive differentiation against cloud-native challengers, and is the dimension with the largest verifiable gap between perceived authority and documentable evidence.

organisations.name: Patterson Companies (Eaglesoft)
organisations.analyst_note: D2 score of 6.0 reflects brand heritage and PDCO institutional signals but risks inflation — no documentable peer-reviewed evidence, named KOLs, or outcomes data with methodology were verifiable; a strict reading of the rubric could support 4.5–5.0. Scored at 6.0 on the basis of verifiable investor-grade corporate credibility materials and market longevity, but this is a boundary case.
organisations.d1_rationale: Eaglesoft.net maintains indexed product pages and a support knowledge base, and Patterson Companies has strong brand search recognition as a legacy vendor; however, category-level searches for 'dental practice management software' and 'cloud dental software' are increasingly dominated by cloud-native competitors whose SEO investment and content velocity outpace Eaglesoft's largely static desktop-product positioning. LinkedIn presence for Patterson Companies is active but Eaglesoft-specific content is sparse and conference discoverability (e.g., Eaglesoft user group presence at ADA) has diminished relative to prior years. Procurement platform listings (e.g., Capterra, G2, Software Advice) exist but Eaglesoft review recency and volume lag behind Curve Dental and Dentrix Ascend.
organisations.d2_rationale: Patterson Companies benefits from decades of institutional credibility as a publicly traded distributor-manufacturer (PDCO, NASDAQ), and Eaglesoft's longevity in the market constitutes an implicit credibility signal; however, no documentable FDA clearance applies to core PMS functionality, and no named peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies attributable specifically to Eaglesoft were verifiable in dental trade literature as of this audit. Named KOL relationships and white papers with methodology and sample size are not independently verified at audit date from available public sources; marketing copy on eaglesoft.net references reliability and customer tenure but does not satisfy the documentable-evidence standard. Credibility rests primarily on brand heritage and PDCO investor materials rather than published clinical or outcomes data.
organisations.d3_rationale: The dual-domain structure (pattersoncompanies.com for corporate, eaglesoft.net for product) creates a coherent but siloed identity; however, messaging consistency across LinkedIn, G2/Capterra listings, and the product site is inconsistent — corporate Patterson messaging emphasizes a broader technology ecosystem (including Fuse and cloud integrations) while eaglesoft.net remains anchored in legacy desktop positioning, creating audience confusion about strategic direction. The worst-integrated case is social channels, where Eaglesoft-specific branding is intermittently surfaced under Patterson corporate handles, and product naming conventions across support forums, reseller pages, and listing platforms show minor but persistent inconsistencies.
organisations.d4_rationale: Pearl — Dental Economics product review positive, 50,000+ clinicians, Capterra positive (minor false positive concerns). PMC systematic review precision/recall exceeding 91%/93%. Inc. Power Partner Award 2025. Active integration announcements across major PMS platforms. No negative record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Henry Schein One (Dentrix/Dentrix Ascend), Open Dental, Curve Dental, and Carestream Dental (as secondary) — Eaglesoft holds a legacy installed-base advantage but trails materially on digital investment trajectory and discoverability signals. Dentrix Ascend and Curve Dental have meaningfully outpaced Eaglesoft on cloud-category SEO, G2/Capterra review recency, and LinkedIn content velocity; Open Dental's open-source model generates strong community-driven organic presence that Eaglesoft cannot replicate. Patterson's public company resources represent a latent competitive asset, but visible digital investment in Eaglesoft-specific digital presence (content marketing, SEO, review cultivation) is below category median, placing Eaglesoft in the bottom half of this competitive set on combined D1–D4 signals.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Exact G2/Capterra ratings and review counts as of April 2026; specific named KOL relationships for Eaglesoft; peer-reviewed study citations attributable to Eaglesoft outcomes. not independently verified at audit date: Glassdoor/Indeed sentiment specific to Eaglesoft product division vs. Patterson Companies corporate. D1 scored conservatively given documented shift of category search intent to cloud-native competitors. D5 competitive set named as: Henry Schein One (Dentrix/Dentrix Ascend), Open Dental, Curve Dental, Carestream Dental. Priority gap selected as D1 over D4 because a 12-month SEO and listing-platform intervention would directly arrest the category discoverability decline driving new-customer pipeline erosion — the highest combined OPS impact available given the competitive trajectory.

organisations.name: Carestream Dental (Sensei Cloud / OrthoTrac)
organisations.analyst_note: CD&R ownership structure and any post-2024 product portfolio rationalisation announcements may have materially changed the legacy product integration picture; training knowledge cutoff means OrthoTrac/Sensei consolidation status as of April 2026 is partially UNVERIFIABLE.
organisations.d1_rationale: Carestream Dental maintains a functional carestreamdental.com with product-level landing pages for Sensei Cloud, OrthoTrac, and imaging hardware, and has LinkedIn presence with regular posting activity. However, trade press discoverability for Sensei Cloud as a cloud PMS is inconsistent — category searches for 'cloud dental practice management software' do not reliably surface Carestream above Henry Schein One or Curve Dental, suggesting SEO investment lags competitors. Procurement platform and G2/Capterra listing completeness is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge and scored conservatively.
organisations.d2_rationale: Carestream Dental holds documentable FDA 510(k) clearances for its imaging portfolio (CS 9600 CBCT series and CS 8200 family carry named clearances publicly on FDA device database), which provides genuine regulatory credibility for the imaging segment. However, Sensei Cloud's clinical outcomes data and peer-reviewed citations are not independently verifiable from training knowledge — no named studies or specific KOL endorsements with active digital presence for the PMS/cloud transition have been identified, and the phrase 'clinically proven' appears in marketing copy without documented methodology. Named KOL relationships for the software stack are not independently verified at audit date and scored conservatively.
organisations.d3_rationale: The brand transition from the legacy Carestream Dental portfolio (which included CS SoftDent, WinOMS, OrthoTrac, Sensei) creates visible coherence risk: multiple legacy product microsites and references remain indexed alongside the Sensei Cloud narrative, and OrthoTrac is marketed under its own identity while also being positioned within the Sensei ecosystem — creating naming ambiguity visible to a prospective buyer doing category research. Social channels (LinkedIn, Twitter/X) do not consistently reflect a unified 'Sensei Cloud-first' message; the worst-integrated case (OrthoTrac standalone versus Sensei Cloud messaging) drives the score down from what the homepage alone would suggest.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dandy — Strong trade press coverage for Vision scanner and DandyCart (Dentistry Today, Globe Newswire). Official review page active. Indeed reviews mixed — positive on mission, negative on micromanagement and rapid expansion quality concerns. LBO by Neem Dec 2025 structural change.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Dentsply Sirona (Axeos/Sidexis imaging, DTX Studio), Planmeca (Romexis, cloud ambitions), Henry Schein One (Dentrix Ascend, Curve via acquisition), and Curve Dental — Carestream Dental holds stronger credibility on imaging hardware D2 than pure-software competitors but is outperformed on D1 discoverability and D4 market voice for the cloud PMS category specifically by Henry Schein One and Curve Dental. Dentsply Sirona's own cloud transition turbulence (documented investor commentary 2024-2025) partially equalises the competitive delta in D3. PE backing from CD&R signals investment capacity but no public digital investment trajectory announcements for Sensei Cloud have been identified that would upgrade the trajectory signal.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: G2/Capterra listing completeness and recency; specific named KOLs for Sensei Cloud PMS with active digital presence; any post-2025 FDA clearances for new imaging products; exact state of OrthoTrac-to-Sensei migration roadmap communications as of April 2026. D2 imaging score is supported by documentable historical FDA clearances (CS 9600, CS 8200 series) but software-side D2 sub-component is scored conservatively at 5.0 internally, pulling the blended D2 to 6.5. Priority gap selected as D3 over D4 because coherence remediation (unified Sensei Cloud narrative, legacy page suppression, OrthoTrac integration messaging) would simultaneously improve D1 SEO signal and D4 review sentiment by reducing buyer confusion — generating higher combined OPS and MI improvement per intervention point than D4 review-response optimisation alone.

organisations.name: Planet DDS (Denticon / Apteryx)
organisations.d1_rationale: planetdds.com ranks competitively for DSO-specific PMS search terms (e.g., 'cloud practice management DSO') and LinkedIn presence is active with regular posting. Denticon and Apteryx as sub-brands are indexed and findable but product-level landing pages and SEO differentiation between the two product lines are inconsistent, creating some discoverability fragmentation; dental trade press coverage (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today) contributes to brand search presence. Conference discoverability at DSO-focused events (DSO Leadership Summit, Group Dentistry Now) is documented but not dominant.
