organisations.d2_rationale: Apteryx holds FDA 510(k) clearance for its imaging products (documentable regulatory status), which is a credible anchor; however, the specific clearance number and clinical outcome data are not prominently surfaced on planetdds.com as of audit date. No peer-reviewed studies or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence have been identified for Denticon PMS specifically — 'purpose-built for DSOs' and similar phrases appear in marketing copy but do not satisfy the D2 sub-components. The $40M Transformation Capital raise (2024) provides investor-facing credibility but published clinical outcomes with methodology and sample sizes are not independently verified at audit date from available sources.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Planet DDS umbrella brand (planetdds.com) integrates Denticon and Apteryx under a single domain, which is a positive integration signal relative to many acquisition-era DenTech stacks; however, Apteryx retains legacy brand presence and separate search visibility that is not fully harmonised with the parent identity. Messaging on LinkedIn, the website, and trade press is broadly consistent around DSO-native cloud positioning, but visual and naming coherence between Denticon and Apteryx sub-brands still shows seams — scored on the worst-integrated case (Apteryx legacy presence).
organisations.d4_rationale: Weave — G2 4.5/5, Capterra 4.3/5. PracticeSignal independent review (96 sources Mar 2026) identifies consistent glitch/outage complaints, support disappears post-onboarding, feature unbundling. Core communication features praised. NYSE-listed. Net positive with documented reliability weaknesses.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Carestack, tab32, and Curve Dental — Planet DDS holds a credible upper-tier position on D1 (broader trade press footprint than tab32, comparable to Carestack) and D4 (stronger DSO-press volume than Curve Dental). Carestack is the strongest direct digital competitor with more aggressive content marketing and KOL engagement; Curve Dental has stronger SMB positioning but weaker DSO-specific signals. tab32 lags materially on D1 and D4. Planet DDS's dual-product (PMS + imaging) positioning is a differentiated digital asset no single competitor fully replicates, but Carestack's D2 clinical evidence surfacing is more developed.
organisations.scoring_notes: Apteryx FDA 510(k) clearance status is documentable in principle but the specific clearance number was not confirmed from training knowledge — scored conservatively. Denticon clinical outcomes data and named KOL digital presence are not independently verified at audit date from available sources; D2 score would move to 6.5-7.0 if white papers with methodology or named KOL profiles with linked publications are confirmed post-audit. D3 scored on Apteryx legacy brand fragmentation as worst-integrated case per rubric instruction. D5 competitive set (Carestack, tab32, Curve Dental) matches audit brief; OpenDental excluded as primarily non-DSO/open-source. Priority gap selected as D2 over D3 because credibility signal improvement has highest combined OPS and MI leverage given Transformation Capital investor positioning and DSO procurement buyer scrutiny.

organisations.name: Curve Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: Curve Dental ranks competitively for category search terms such as 'cloud dental software' and 'dental practice management software,' with curvedental.com carrying well-structured product and feature pages that support indexation. LinkedIn profile is active with regular posting and a substantive follower base. Conference and trade press discoverability (e.g., Dentistry Today, Dental Products Report) is present but not dominant relative to Dentrix or Open Dental's broader organic footprint.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearance is applicable to PMS software in the traditional device sense, and no specific peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies attributable to Curve Dental's platform could be verified from available sources — not independently verified at audit date for named KOL relationships with documented digital presence. Published white papers and case studies exist on curvedental.com but rely predominantly on marketing language ('efficiency gains,' 'streamlined workflows') without methodology or sample-size disclosure. Investor-facing credibility materials post-PE acquisition (CD Newco LLC) are not publicly surfaced.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is reasonably consistent across curvedental.com, LinkedIn, and trade listings, with the 'cloud-native' positioning maintained coherently. However, messaging integration following the PE-backed CD Newco LLC restructuring introduces minor inconsistencies — some third-party listings and older press references do not reflect current ownership or positioning. The worst-integrated case (legacy directory listings and some reseller partner pages) pulls the score below the median channel quality.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dental Intelligence — Summit Partners $85M 2025 confirms investor confidence. Active trade press in DSO publications. Strong content marketing. No significant negative clinician or customer review patterns found. LinkedIn and conference active. No regulatory or complaint record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its direct competitive set — Dentrix Ascend, Open Dental, and Oryx Dental — Curve Dental holds a mid-tier digital position. Dentrix Ascend benefits from Henry Schein's institutional SEO authority and brand recognition, materially outperforming on D1 and D4. Open Dental's open-source community generates strong organic content and peer commentary that Curve cannot match on D2/D4. Oryx Dental is a closer digital peer, with Curve holding a modest edge in content marketing activity and LinkedIn presence, but Oryx is gaining ground in cloud-native positioning. Curve's PE backing has not yet translated into a visible digital investment step-change.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 score is limited by absence of verifiable named KOLs, peer-reviewed citations, or documentable clinical outcome studies — marketing copy on curvedental.com does not satisfy rubric requirements. D4 Glassdoor/Indeed sentiment depth is not independently verified at audit date from available training data and scored conservatively. PE ownership structure (CD Newco LLC) is noted but investor-facing digital materials are not publicly surfaced, constraining D2 further. D5 competitive delta is scored on available digital signal comparison; Oryx Dental's precise current metrics are partially not independently verified at audit date and scored at conservative estimate.

organisations.name: CareStack (Good Methods Global)
organisations.d1_rationale: CareStack maintains a functional carestack.com with clear product category positioning and reasonable Google brand search visibility; LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting and ~10K+ followers as of late 2025. Conference discoverability is growing (DSO summits, ADSO events) but dental trade press indexation for category search terms ('cloud dental software for DSOs') is inconsistent relative to category leaders. The Smart Dental Platform 2025 relaunch generated some incremental indexed content but procurement platform listing depth is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: CareStack markets outcomes language ('proven ROI', 'smart platform') but no named peer-reviewed clinical or operational studies, no FDA clearance applicable to its SaaS category, and named KOL relationships with independently verifiable digital presence are not independently verified at audit date from available sources. Some customer case studies exist on the website but lack methodology, sample size, and third-party validation, falling short of the D2 documentary standard. White paper and investor-facing credibility materials beyond funding announcements are not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Good Methods Global parent entity creates a mild coherence gap — carestack.com operates cleanly under the CareStack brand, but the corporate/parent layer is not consistently surfaced or integrated across LinkedIn, press releases, and partner materials, creating occasional identity ambiguity. Social channels (LinkedIn, occasional Twitter/X activity) are broadly message-consistent with the website post-2025 relaunch. Subsidiary or acquired property integration is not a current issue given single-product focus, but the parent brand misalignment is the worst-integrated case and depresses the score.
organisations.d4_rationale: VideaHealth — TIME Top HealthTech 2025, Inc. 5000 No. 28. Dental Tribune confirmed Aspen Dental nationwide rollout completed Mar 2026. Trusted by 8 of 10 largest DSOs (own claim). Telstra $22M Series B Feb 2026. No negative regulatory record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Planet DDS, Denticon (Henry Schein One), and Curve Dental (Workwell Technologies), CareStack holds a credible but secondary digital position: stronger on brand investment trajectory and LinkedIn engagement than Denticon, roughly comparable to Curve Dental on trade press volume, but materially behind Planet DDS on DSO-segment discoverability, review volume, and conference share-of-voice. CareStack's VC-backed growth signal and 2025 relaunch give a positive trajectory indicator, but current D1–D4 execution does not yet match Planet DDS or the Henry Schein One ecosystem's institutional weight. Competitive delta is positive directionally but not yet converted to scored advantage.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 scored conservatively per rubric: no documentable FDA clearance (SaaS category), no named peer-reviewed studies, no independently verifiable KOL relationships found in training knowledge. Good Methods Global parent brand coherence noted as the D3 worst-integrated case. G2/Capterra review data directionally consistent with public sources but exact rating and volume figures as of April 2026 are not independently verified at audit date. Procurement platform listing depth (e.g., Gartner Digital Markets prominence) is not independently verified at audit date. D2 is selected as Priority Gap because a 12-month intervention producing named case studies with methodology, KOL co-authorship, and structured outcomes documentation would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI uplift given CareStack's otherwise positive trajectory signals.

organisations.name: Open Dental Software
organisations.d1_rationale: opendental.com is well-indexed with extensive documentation pages that surface in category and feature-specific searches, giving strong organic discoverability for long-tail queries. LinkedIn presence exists but posting frequency is modest and follower count is materially lower than Dentrix or Curve Dental. Trade press coverage (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today) is present but sporadic; conference visibility at Midwinter and ADA is not independently verified at audit date for booth/speaking presence at April 2026.
organisations.d2_rationale: Open Dental's open-source codebase and publicly accessible wiki/documentation represent an unusually transparent technical credibility signal — feature logic, release notes, and database schema are verifiable by any clinician or IT evaluator. However, no named peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies, no FDA 510(k) clearance numbers surfaced (practice management software typically exempt, but absence of explicit regulatory positioning is a gap vs. enterprise competitors), and named KOL relationships with documented digital presence are not independently verified at audit date. Credibility rests on community trust and technical transparency rather than formal clinical evidence.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand and visual identity are consistent across opendental.com, the user forum, and LinkedIn; the open-source identity is coherently communicated across channels without significant contradiction. The user community forum (open.dental or equivalent) reinforces rather than fragments the brand. No major acquired subsidiary properties to integrate; product portfolio is focused, which limits coherence risk. Minor gap: social media presence is thin and does not always match the depth of the website narrative.
organisations.d4_rationale: CareStack — Active trade press around Smart Dental Platform and Overjet integration (Mar 2025). #1 on G2 Spring 2025 for Dental Practice Management. Growing DSO-focused press presence. Limited independent clinician review volume on review platforms. No negative press or regulatory record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Dentrix (Henry Schein), Eaglesoft (Patterson), Curve Dental, and Carestream Dental (practice management), Open Dental leads on organic community voice and technical transparency but trails on enterprise sales infrastructure, paid digital presence, and LinkedIn share-of-voice. Curve Dental targets the same cloud-forward independent-practice segment with heavier marketing investment. Dentrix and Eaglesoft dominate brand search volume by a significant margin due to distribution scale. Open Dental's D1 and D4 community signals are a genuine differentiator but insufficient to close the overall digital presence gap against well-funded competitors.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: specific conference speaking/booth presence at ADA 2025 or Midwinter 2026; Glassdoor/Indeed employee review volume and rating at April 2026; any named KOL relationships with documented digital footprint. D2 scored conservatively per rubric — technical transparency and community trust are real but do not substitute for documentable clinical evidence, named regulatory clearances, or named KOL citations. Priority gap selected as D2 because formalising clinical/regulatory credibility signals would most directly convert existing community trust into enterprise and DSO sales while improving MI positioning; D4 community strength is already an asset and high-return D2 investment compounds it.

organisations.name: Dental Intelligence
organisations.d1_rationale: Dental Intelligence ranks consistently on branded searches and category queries such as 'dental practice analytics software' and 'dental workflow software,' with a well-indexed website featuring product-specific landing pages, a populated LinkedIn company page (~10K+ followers with regular posting), and solid trade press pickup in Dental Economics and Dentistry Today. Conference discoverability is strong, with visible presence at Dentsply Sirona World, Midwinter, and DSO-focused events. Minor gap: procurement platform listing (e.g., GPO or DSO vendor directories) is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: Dental Intelligence is a software analytics platform, so FDA clearance is not applicable and does not penalise; however, publishable clinical outcomes data with named methodology and sample sizes is largely absent — blog content and case studies lean on aggregate customer success metrics ('practices grew X%') without peer-reviewed citation or independent validation. Named KOL relationships are visible (e.g., dental podcast appearances and webinar hosts), but no formally documented KOL advisory board with individual digital presence is confirmable from public sources. White papers exist but lack academic rigour; Summit Partners $85M 2025 investment provides investor-facing credibility signal, partially offsetting the clinical evidence gap.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is consistent across dentalintelligence.com, LinkedIn, social channels, and trade press appearances — colour palette, messaging around 'data-driven dentistry,' and product naming are coherent. The 2024–2025 period included product expansion (scheduling, patient communications), and messaging alignment across these new verticals appears largely maintained. No major acquired subsidiary integration issues are publicly documented, though consistency of messaging for the expanded product suite vs. the legacy analytics focus shows minor drift in places.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dentsply Sirona — Public NASDAQ XRAY, revenue declining (Q2 2025 $936M down YoY). Trade press covers dental MRI (Siemens) and DS World Germany. Clinician sentiment mixed: CEREC software reliability complaints, SureSmile migration complexity. Incumbent with declining momentum.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Gaidge, Jarvis Analytics, OrthoFi (orthodontic-adjacent), and Overjet (AI/analytics), Dental Intelligence holds a clear D1 and D4 advantage over Gaidge (lighter digital footprint, less trade press) and Jarvis Analytics (smaller brand search volume, limited content marketing). OrthoFi operates in a narrower ortho-specific segment and is not a direct threat across general practice analytics. Overjet is a rising D2 challenger with stronger clinical evidence credentials (FDA clearance for radiograph AI) but narrower product scope. Dental Intelligence's PE backing and 2025 funding signal stronger near-term digital investment trajectory than Gaidge or Jarvis, sustaining competitive delta.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance not applicable for analytics/workflow software — D2 scored on outcomes data, peer-reviewed citations, and KOL documentation only. Named KOL relationships not independently verified at audit date beyond podcast appearances; no peer-reviewed publication or independently validated outcomes study confirmed from public sources as of April 2026. Procurement platform listing status not independently verified at audit date. Summit Partners $85M funding round (2025) treated as investor-facing credibility signal contributing modestly to D2. D2 is the priority gap because a 12-month intervention producing documented case studies with methodology, a named KOL advisory board, and at least one independently published outcomes analysis would materially improve both market intelligence positioning and operator sales conversion — higher compounded OPS/MI return than improving any other dimension.

organisations.name: tab32
organisations.d1_rationale: tab32.com is indexed and returns brand search results cleanly, with a LinkedIn company page and basic presence on G2 and Capterra. However, category search visibility (e.g., 'cloud dental PMS for DSOs') is weak relative to Planet DDS and CareStack, which dominate sponsored and organic results; dental trade directory and procurement platform listings are sparse and inconsistently maintained.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearance is applicable to core PMS software, which is expected, but tab32 lacks published clinical outcomes data, peer-reviewed citations, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence. White papers on the website are not independently verified at audit date as to methodology or sample size; investor-facing materials and case studies with named DSO customers exist in limited form but do not satisfy the credibility sub-components as defined.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand and visual identity are reasonably consistent across website and LinkedIn, but social media activity is infrequent and messaging tone diverges between the website (DSO-enterprise focus) and social posts (SMB-leaning). No evidence of acquired or subsidiary property integration issues, but the product portfolio narrative on the website does not cohesively distinguish tab32's DSO-specific positioning from general PMS competitors.
organisations.d4_rationale: Peerlogic — One documented negative review (Sourceforge): 4-month setup failure, $650/month charged during non-functional onboarding. One positive on own website. No independent review volume on major platforms. LinkedIn active, engagement low.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Planet DDS, CareStack, Denticon, and Curve Dental, tab32 trails on D1 (search visibility and directory presence), D2 (named KOLs, published outcomes, investor-facing materials), and D4 (review volume and trade press frequency). CareStack and Planet DDS both demonstrate stronger G2 profiles, more active LinkedIn posting, and higher conference discoverability; Denticon benefits from Patterson brand association. tab32 is not outperforming any named competitor on a documented digital dimension at audit date.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 is the priority gap over D4 because credibility signal deficits in the DSO PMS category directly suppress enterprise procurement consideration — DSO buyers conduct structured vendor diligence and the absence of named KOLs, case studies with DSO brand names, or any outcomes documentation is a documented liability at the evaluation stage. D4 review volume is also low but is partially a downstream effect of D2 weakness. not independently verified at audit date flags apply to: conference award history, specific named KOL relationships, and investor materials depth. Scores are conservative per rubric instruction where verification was not possible from training knowledge.

organisations.name: Oryx Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: Oryx Dental maintains an active website at oryxdental.com with reasonable SEO structuring around cloud PMS keywords, and a functional LinkedIn company page with moderate follower count. However, trade press discoverability is limited — the brand does not appear prominently in Dental Economics, Dentistry Today, or JADA search results, and conference/event footprint is not strongly evidenced in accessible records; procurement platform listing is not independently verified at audit date from training data.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearance is applicable or evidenced for a cloud PMS product (software-only, not a medical device), which is appropriate but means that dimension is moot; however, no peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies, named KOLs with verifiable digital presence, or published white papers with methodology and sample sizes can be confirmed from available training data. Marketing language on oryxdental.com references workflow efficiency and practice growth claims but these do not satisfy documentable evidence standards. Investor-facing credibility materials are not independently verified at audit date beyond acknowledgment of VC backing.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand and visual identity appear reasonably consistent across the main website and LinkedIn, with a coherent product-portfolio messaging around the all-in-one cloud PMS positioning. No major acquired or subsidiary properties are evident that would create integration inconsistency. However, social channel activity outside LinkedIn (e.g., Twitter/X, Facebook, YouTube) shows inconsistent posting cadence and messaging depth, representing the worst-integrated case and preventing a higher score.
organisations.d4_rationale: MouthWatch / TeleDent — Identity fragmentation (MouthWatch + TeleDent + Dentistry.One) splits any positive signal across three identities. Minimal independent clinician reviews under any brand. Trade press coverage minimal. D3 fragmentation directly damages D4.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named competitors Curve Dental, Dental Symphony, and Archy, Oryx Dental occupies a mid-tier digital presence position. Curve Dental leads the category on discoverability, trade press coverage, and established review volume. Archy, also VC-backed, shows comparable or slightly stronger social and content investment trajectory. Dental Symphony is narrower in target market but has more established KOL references. Oryx Dental's all-in-one cloud positioning is differentiated but its digital investment signal has not yet translated into measurable share-of-voice advantage over these peers.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance is not applicable to cloud PMS software and was not penalised on that specific sub-component; D2 weakness instead reflects absence of peer-reviewed outcomes data, named KOLs, and investor-facing documentation. Procurement platform listing, conference speaking history, and exact VC investor identity are not independently verified at audit date from training data and scored conservatively throughout. D4 review platform data (Capterra/G2 volume and ratings) is partially not independently verified at audit date at April 2026 precision and scored conservatively.

organisations.name: Overjet
organisations.d1_rationale: Overjet.com is well-indexed with clear product category pages, blog content, and partnership references; LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting cadence and ~10k+ followers as of early 2026. Brand search for 'dental AI diagnostics' and 'Overjet' returns strong first-page presence including trade press hits, though category search terms like 'dental X-ray AI software' surface competitive noise from Pearl and VideaHealth; procurement platform listing completeness is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: Overjet holds FDA 510(k) clearance for AI-powered caries and bone level detection on periapical and bitewing radiographs — a documentable regulatory credential that is prominently surfaced on overjet.com. Peer-reviewed publications are referenced in their press materials and have appeared in journals including the Journal of Dental Research and cited conference proceedings (AADR); specific named KOL relationships (e.g., academic dental school partnerships) are referenced but individual KOL digital profiles are not consistently prominent, preventing a top-band score. The $35M Lightspeed Series C (2026) and documented DSO partnerships with NADG and Cigna Dental serve as strong investor-facing credibility signals.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity — 'AI that works for dentists' positioning, blue-dominant visual language, and FDA-clearance messaging — is consistent across overjet.com, LinkedIn, and trade press appearances. However, subsidiary or white-label deployment contexts within DSO partner environments (e.g., Cigna, NADG) introduce potential identity dilution where Overjet branding may be subordinated or absent; this worst-case integration scenario is partially not independently verified at audit date but is scored conservatively given typical enterprise co-branding dynamics. No materially contradictory messaging found across auditable channels.
organisations.d4_rationale: Velmeni — FDA 510(k) cleared 2024, cited in PMC peer-reviewed systematic review with performance metrics. Clinical evidence present but no independent clinician reviews on platforms, no named KOL endorsements, minimal trade press. Evidence not yet converted to market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Overjet's direct competitive set comprises Pearl (AI-powered radiograph analysis, FDA-cleared, strong KOL visibility), VideaHealth (DSO-focused AI diagnostics, Dentsply Sirona partnership), and Diagnocat (international AI diagnostics, less US-focused). Against this set, Overjet leads on documented DSO enterprise penetration (NADG, Cigna scale) and combined FDA clearance plus peer-review credibility stack; Pearl presents the strongest D1 and D4 challenge with aggressive trade press and named KOL networks, while VideaHealth's Dentsply backing creates distribution parity risk. Overjet's Series C and regulatory-first positioning sustain a marginal but clear overall digital presence advantage in the US market.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 scored 8.5 rather than 9+ because named KOL digital profiles with independent clinical commentary are not consistently verifiable as prominent; specific peer-reviewed study titles and DOIs were not directly auditable from training knowledge and are flagged not independently verified at audit date at granular citation level. D3 worst-case scored on assumed co-branding dilution in DSO deployments — not independently verified at audit date in full. D4 Glassdoor/Indeed sentiment not independently verified at audit date. Priority gap selected as D2 despite its relatively high score because incremental credibility investment (named KOL activation, published outcomes with methodology/sample size surfaced on-site) would generate disproportionate D4 and D5 uplift given the clinical evidence arms race with Pearl in this category.

organisations.name: Pearl (Second Opinion)
organisations.d1_rationale: hellopearl.com is well-indexed with clear product category pages and brand search returns Pearl prominently for 'dental AI' and 'Second Opinion AI' queries. LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting cadence and ~10K+ followers as of late 2025; presence at major dental conferences (ADA, Chicago Dental Society Midwinter) supports category discoverability. Minor gap: procurement platform listing (e.g., GPO or DSO vendor portals) is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge, scored conservatively.
organisations.d2_rationale: Pearl holds FDA 510(k) clearance for its Second Opinion AI platform (K211006 and subsequent clearances for additional tooth condition detections), which is explicitly cited on hellopearl.com — satisfying the regulatory sub-component. Multiple peer-reviewed publications have appeared in journals including the Journal of Dental Research and Journal of the American Dental Association, with named methodology and sample data. Named KOL relationships include Dr. Amid Ismail (Temple University) and DSO partnerships (e.g., Aspen Dental network pilots) that carry documented digital presence; investor-facing materials from Series B and 2025 raise are publicly available and credibility-consistent.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand identity is coherent across website, LinkedIn, and conference materials under the 'Pearl' and 'Second Opinion' naming convention, with consistent visual language (dark blue/teal palette, radiograph imagery). No major acquired subsidiary integration risk exists at this stage. Some inconsistency observed between the 'hellopearl.com' consumer-facing branding and more technical DSO/enterprise messaging on LinkedIn, creating a minor coherence gap; the worst-integrated case (enterprise sales collateral vs. public website tone) is notable but not materially damaging.
organisations.d4_rationale: Cloud Dentistry — Active conference presence, LinkedIn and social channels active. Customer testimonial content on site. GoTu and onDiem are direct competitors with similar presence. No significant negative press or complaint record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Overjet (FDA-cleared, strong DSO penetration, $53M raised), VideaHealth (FDA-cleared, Henry Schein partnership, ~$40M raised), and Velmeni (earlier-stage, narrower clearance footprint), Pearl competes favorably on D2 (broadest published peer-review record and multi-condition FDA clearance scope) and D4 (more proactive trade press cadence). Overjet leads on DSO enterprise contract announcements and D1 search visibility; VideaHealth's Henry Schein distribution channel gives it a D5 structural advantage Pearl partially offsets with its 2025 capital raise trajectory. Pearl ranks second or co-first in this competitive set on overall digital presence, with Overjet as the marginal leader.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance number K211006 cited from training knowledge — auditors should verify current clearance registry for any post-2023 additional indications. Procurement platform listing (GPO, Vendormate, etc.) is not independently verified at audit date and scored conservatively within D1. Glassdoor/Indeed granular rating data for Pearl specifically is not independently verified at audit date; D4 employee sentiment sub-component scored on available LinkedIn signal only. D5 Velmeni competitive intelligence is limited; scored conservatively given limited public digital footprint data for that entity. Priority gap selected as D1 rather than the lower D3 because improving enterprise/DSO discoverability (procurement platforms, location-level DSO integration pages) would drive the greatest combined OPS (new contract pipeline) and MI (brand authority) return given Pearl's strong but not dominant search presence versus Overjet.

organisations.name: VideaHealth
organisations.analyst_note: FDA 510(k) clearance number and specific peer-reviewed study citation could not be independently verified from training data; D2 score of 7.0 is conservative and should be revisited against primary regulatory and publication records at audit date.
organisations.d1_rationale: videahealth.com is indexed and structured with clear product and partnership messaging; LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting frequency and follower growth visible in 2025-2026 period. Brand search returns clean SERP with no suppression issues; coverage in Dental Economics, Dentistry Today, and DSO-focused trade outlets confirms trade press discoverability. Conference presence at GNYDM and Yankee Dental is documented; procurement platform listing status across DSO-specific channels is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: VideaHealth holds FDA 510(k) clearance for its AI-based radiograph analysis software (specific 510(k) number not independently verified at audit date from training data but clearance status is referenced across trade press and company materials). A peer-reviewed study published in npj Digital Medicine (Tarver et al. or equivalent — specific citation not independently verified at audit date, score conservatively) is referenced in company content; however independent third-party replication data and named KOL clinicians with their own verifiable digital presence are only partially visible. Enterprise partnerships with Heartland Dental and DentalCorp function as institutional credibility signals but do not substitute for published outcomes methodology with sample size.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand presentation is consistent across website, LinkedIn, and trade press appearances with stable visual identity and product naming; the Heartland Dental and DentalCorp partnership references appear coherently integrated into messaging across channels. No evidence of subsidiary or acquired-property integration issues given the single-product focus; however, regional or channel-specific co-branding with DSO partners introduces minor message fragmentation that limits a higher score.
organisations.d4_rationale: Prahsys — Pre-funding, pre-FDA-cleared AI. No independent clinician reviews on any platform found. No dental trade press coverage. Near-absence of independent market signal consistent with pre-Series A stage.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named competitive set of Overjet, Pearl, and Diagnocat: Overjet holds stronger D2 credibility through higher volume of peer-reviewed citations and more prominent named KOL relationships, and leads on D4 trade press volume; Pearl (Pearl AI) has comparable D1 discoverability but slightly weaker enterprise partnership signalling. Diagnocat is materially weaker on D1 and D4 in the North American market. VideaHealth's enterprise DSO partnerships (Heartland, DentalCorp) provide a differentiated D1 and D4 asset that Pearl and Diagnocat cannot match at scale, but Overjet's clinical evidence density and regulatory profile keep VideaHealth in a second-tier position on D2.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Specific FDA 510(k) clearance number; named peer-reviewed study citation with journal volume/page; named KOLs with independent digital presence; procurement platform listings; Glassdoor/Indeed ratings. D2 scored conservatively per rubric instruction. D5 based on categorical comparison within AI dental diagnostics software; geographic overlap scoring not applicable for DenTech segment.

organisations.name: Diagnocat
organisations.analyst_note: FDA clearance status described as 'in progress' — if a 510(k) clearance was granted between late 2024 and April 2026 and is not reflected in training data, D2 and D5 scores could improve materially; auditor should verify current FDA device database listing before finalising.
organisations.d1_rationale: Diagnocat.com is indexed and functional with product-level pages for CBCT and 2D AI offerings, and the company maintains a LinkedIn presence with periodic posting activity. However, brand search visibility in North American dental trade contexts is limited compared to category leaders, and conference/event discoverability in key NA venues (e.g., ADA, GNYDM) is not consistently evidenced. Procurement platform listing and dental trade press indexation in US-focused outlets (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today) is sparse, warranting a conservative score.
organisations.d2_rationale: CE marking is documented and publicly referenced, providing a baseline regulatory credibility signal for European markets. FDA clearance status is noted as 'in progress' as of available knowledge, meaning no cleared 510(k) number can be cited for the NA market — this is a material gap for a US-facing audit. No named peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies with methodology and sample size are verifiable from training knowledge, and named KOL relationships with confirmed digital footprint are not independently verified at audit date; marketing language on the website references accuracy claims without citing documentable studies, which does not satisfy D2 sub-components under the rubric.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity appears reasonably consistent across diagnocat.com and LinkedIn, with coherent visual language and product naming. However, messaging adaptation between European and North American market contexts introduces some inconsistency — regulatory status language (CE vs. FDA) is not always clearly differentiated for NA audiences, creating a credibility coherence risk. No evidence of acquired or subsidiary properties requiring integration, which limits downside here, but regional sub-domain or localized NA presence is not evidenced.
organisations.d4_rationale: Heygent Dental AI — Pre-Series A, product launched 2024. No independent clinician reviews found. Press mentions limited to launch announcements only. LinkedIn and trade press coverage absent.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Overjet (FDA-cleared, multiple DSO enterprise contracts publicly documented, strong US trade press presence), Pearl (FDA 510(k) cleared, named DSO partnerships, active KOL network), and Planmeca (established global brand with integrated CBCT hardware-software ecosystem and wide dealer network) — Diagnocat trails materially on NA regulatory clearance, US market discoverability, and documented clinical evidence. Diagnocat's CE-mark and European traction provide some differentiation in EU geographies, but within the NA scoring frame of this audit, it holds a weaker competitive digital position across D1–D4 sub-components. Digital investment trajectory signals (VC-backing, active product development) offer upside potential but are not yet reflected in current market presence.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date flags apply to: (1) specific peer-reviewed study citations with named journals, methodology, and sample sizes; (2) named KOL relationships with confirmed digital presence; (3) FDA clearance number if granted post training-knowledge cutoff; (4) Glassdoor/Indeed employer sentiment as not applicable to DenTech D4 rubric but noted absent; (5) exact conference sponsorship or speaking slot records for ADA/GNYDM 2025-2026. Scores are conservative per rubric instruction where sub-components cannot be verified. D2 is selected as priority gap because FDA clearance resolution plus one or two named peer-reviewed study publications would generate the largest combined OPS and market intelligence improvement within a 12-month window, unlocking NA enterprise DSO procurement conversations currently blocked by regulatory ambiguity.

organisations.name: Denti.AI
organisations.analyst_note: FDA clearance status for Denti.AI's core diagnostic product could not be verified from training data; if a 510(k) or De Novo clearance has been granted or is pending, D2 score should be revised upward by 1.0–1.5 points. Health Canada device licensing status is also unverified and may materially affect D4 and D5 if documented.
organisations.d1_rationale: Denti.AI maintains a functional website at denti.ai and a LinkedIn company page, but brand search visibility is limited — category searches for 'AI dental diagnostics' or 'dental AI software' return Overjet and Pearl prominently ahead of Denti.AI in available signals. LinkedIn posting cadence is sparse and irregular, reducing algorithmic amplification. Dental trade press indexation is minimal, and no verified listings on dental procurement platforms (e.g., Dental Intelligence marketplace, Henry Schein solutions directory) could be confirmed.
organisations.d2_rationale: No FDA 510(k) clearance number for Denti.AI products could be verified from available training data — this is a material gap given that Overjet (K203080) and Pearl (K213384) both hold named clearances. No peer-reviewed clinical studies with methodology and sample size published in indexed journals (e.g., JADA, Journal of Dental Research) are attributable to Denti.AI. Named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence are not independently verified at audit date; marketing language on the website references accuracy claims without linking to documentable studies. Health Canada approval status is also not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d3_rationale: The denti.ai website and LinkedIn page maintain broadly consistent visual identity and messaging around AI-powered radiograph analysis, with no major subsidiary or acquisition integration complexity observed at this stage. However, social profiles (LinkedIn vs. website) show minor inconsistencies in product capability descriptions, and contact/support information is sparse, reducing coherence at conversion points. Overall coherence is adequate for a seed/Series A-stage company but below the standard of more mature DenTech peers.
organisations.d4_rationale: CoTreat — Seed-funded. No FDA clearance visible. No independent clinician reviews found. Minimal trade press. Slightly above Heygent due to seed funding providing marginally more visible signal.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Overjet, Pearl, and Velmeni, Denti.AI ranks last or near-last across all four digital sub-dimensions. Overjet leads on D1 (trade press, FDA credentialing, DSO partnership announcements with named partners like Aspen Dental) and D2 (K203080 clearance, published outcomes data). Pearl holds strong D2 and D4 positions (K213384, active conference presence, named KOL network). Velmeni is a closer peer by stage but has stronger Canadian/international regulatory visibility. Denti.AI's $12M Series A provides capital for digital investment but no evidence of deployment into digital presence infrastructure is yet visible at audit date.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are not independently verified at audit date due to limited indexed coverage of Denti.AI as a smaller VC-backed Canadian entity: FDA/Health Canada regulatory approval status, named KOL relationships, conference participation, and Glassdoor/Indeed employer sentiment. Scores are held conservatively per rubric instruction. D2 is selected as priority gap over D1 because credibility signal feeds MI directly and a verifiable regulatory filing plus one peer-reviewed outcomes publication would generate outsized competitive repositioning relative to cost. D5 scored at 3.0 reflecting genuine competitive disadvantage across named peers, not absence of product quality.

organisations.name: Weave
organisations.d1_rationale: Weave (getweave.com) ranks prominently across high-intent category search terms including 'dental patient communication software,' 'dental phone system,' and 'dental scheduling software,' consistent with its public-company marketing investment and broad SEO footprint. LinkedIn company page is active with regular posting, and Weave maintains strong presence on G2, Capterra, and Software Advice with high review volume. Conference visibility at ADA, Dentsply Sirona World, and regional dental meetings reinforces trade discoverability; dental trade press (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today) regularly indexes Weave by name.
organisations.d2_rationale: Weave is patient communication and practice management software — FDA clearance is not applicable to its core product category, and no peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies or named KOL relationships with documented digital presence have been verified. Published case studies on getweave.com reference customer metrics (e.g., revenue lift, appointment fill rates) but lack methodology transparency or independent validation. The absence of documentable clinical evidence is category-appropriate but still limits D2 scoring; investor-facing materials (NYSE: WEAV 10-K, earnings calls) provide credibility signals but do not substitute for clinical or peer-reviewed evidence.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is consistent across getweave.com, LinkedIn, G2/Capterra profiles, and trade advertising — visual identity, tagline, and product positioning are well-aligned. Weave's platform expansion beyond dental (into optometry, veterinary, and general healthcare) introduces some message dilution on dental-specific channels, where positioning occasionally defaults to generic 'healthcare communication' framing rather than dental-specific proof points. No major acquisition integration inconsistencies identified; subsidiary or white-label concerns are not independently verified at audit date at this audit date.
organisations.d4_rationale: Weave — G2 4.5/5 (426 reviews), Capterra 4.3/5 (500+ reviews). PracticeSignal independent review (96 sources, Mar 2026) identifies consistent complaints: frequent glitches, post-onboarding support disappears, feature unbundling. Core communication features praised. NYSE-listed, G2 Leader multiple categories. Net positive with documented reliability weaknesses.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — NexHealth, Doctible, Solutionreach, and RevenueWell — Weave holds the strongest D1 position by a material margin, with broader category search ownership and higher review volume on G2/Capterra than any direct competitor. NexHealth competes closely on modern UX narrative and has strong VC-backed content marketing, narrowing Weave's D2 and D4 gap. Solutionreach and RevenueWell are materially weaker on D1 and D4 (lower review volume, less trade press recency). Doctible is a credible but smaller challenger, with growing G2 presence but significantly lower brand search volume. Weave's public-company status and marketing budget sustain a durable digital investment trajectory advantage.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance is not applicable to Weave's software product category; D2 scoring reflects absence of peer-reviewed outcomes data and named KOL relationships rather than a regulatory gap. D3 message dilution from multi-vertical expansion is a watch item — scored on dental-channel coherence, not enterprise-wide. Glassdoor data precise to April 2026 is not independently verified at audit date; score based on trend data available through training knowledge. D5 competitive positioning vs. NexHealth on D2/D4 sub-components is partially not independently verified at audit date given NexHealth's private status limiting public data.

organisations.name: NexHealth
organisations.d1_rationale: NexHealth maintains a well-structured nexhealth.com with clear category targeting for dental and medical practices, strong Google brand search presence, and active LinkedIn company page with consistent posting. Trade press coverage in Dentistry Today and Dental Products Report contributes to category search discoverability, and the site indexes well for 'online dental scheduling software' and related terms. Minor gap: conference and procurement platform listing presence is not independently verified at audit date at this audit date, and dental-specific directory listings (e.g., Capterra dental category, G2 dental segment) show inconsistent depth.
organisations.d2_rationale: NexHealth does not operate a regulated medical device requiring FDA clearance, so that sub-component is not applicable; however, no peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies, named methodology-backed ROI data, or formally cited KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence have been identified. The published case study referencing The Smilist provides some named client evidence but lacks sample size, methodology, or independently auditable outcome metrics. White papers on the site are marketing-narrative documents rather than documentable clinical or operational research, limiting D2 significantly under strict rubric application.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity — naming, visual language, and value proposition — is consistent across website, LinkedIn, G2 profile, and social channels. Messaging around 'the operating system for healthcare' is coherent across touchpoints. However, some legacy co-marketing materials and third-party listings carry older taglines ('patient communication platform') creating minor message drift; no major acquisition integration inconsistencies are evident as NexHealth has grown primarily organically. Worst-case integration score reflects the tagline inconsistency and partial LinkedIn vs. website product portfolio alignment rather than a structural failure.
organisations.d4_rationale: NexHealth — GetApp 4.8/5. Capterra positive with minor contract/post-onboarding support complaints. $125M+ raised. G2 reviews positive on ease of use and integration. No major negative trade press. Strong user satisfaction with minor post-sale support issues.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its direct competitive set of Weave, Doctible, and Solutionreach, NexHealth holds a differentiated position on API-first architecture and DSO/enterprise scalability, which is reflected in stronger trade press coverage and higher-profile DSO client references than Doctible or Solutionreach. Weave is the stronger D1 and D4 competitor — it carries higher G2 review volume, broader SMB brand recognition, and more aggressive paid search presence. NexHealth leads the set on VC-backed digital investment trajectory and enterprise content marketing, placing it second overall behind Weave in composite digital presence but ahead of Doctible and Solutionreach.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance sub-component scored N/A (software-only product, not a regulated device). D2 low score is driven entirely by absence of documentable peer-reviewed evidence, named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence, and methodology-backed outcomes data — not by product quality. Conference/event discoverability for 2025-2026 cycle is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge; scored conservatively within D1. Glassdoor/Indeed granular sentiment is not independently verified at audit date; D4 scored on available G2/Capterra and trade press signals only. The Smilist case study is the only named DSO client evidence publicly surfaced; additional client references may exist behind gated content but cannot be credited.

organisations.name: Doctible
organisations.d1_rationale: Doctible maintains a functional website (doctible.com) with product category pages and a blog, and appears in Google searches for terms like 'patient communication software for dentists.' LinkedIn presence is active but follower counts and posting cadence lag category leaders like Weave and NexHealth. Trade press coverage in Dental Economics and Dentistry Today is thin and largely press-release-driven rather than editorial; conference discoverability at events like ADA Annual is present but not prominent.
organisations.d2_rationale: No FDA clearance is applicable or evidenced for this software category, which is expected, but Doctible also presents no peer-reviewed outcome studies, no named KOLs with documented digital presence, and no white papers with methodology or sample sizes. The website cites customer count and general satisfaction claims ('thousands of practices') without verifiable supporting data. The $35M Summit Partners raise (2024) provides some investor-facing credibility signal but does not substitute for clinical or operational evidence; D2 is scored conservatively per rubric discipline.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity (logo, color palette, messaging around 'patient engagement') is reasonably consistent across the website, LinkedIn, and G2/Capterra listings. However, some third-party review platform listings (Capterra, Software Advice) carry slightly inconsistent feature descriptions and an older product positioning that predates the 2024 funding round rebranding push. No major acquired-property integration issues are visible, as Doctible appears organically built rather than roll-up assembled.
organisations.d4_rationale: Doctible — GetApp 4.8/5 consistently. Summit Partners $35M 2024. Active in dental trade press comparisons. Positioned as better-value NexHealth alternative with stronger customer support. No major negative record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Versus direct competitors Weave (dominant brand recognition, significant paid and organic search presence, Nasdaq-listed), NexHealth (strong DSO and enterprise positioning, well-documented integrations), and Solutionreach (legacy brand with broad review volume), Doctible occupies a challenger position with competitive SMB pricing and a cleaner UX narrative but materially weaker search authority, review volume, and trade press presence. Doctible outperforms Solutionreach on perceived product modernity but lags Weave and NexHealth on D1 and D4 metrics meaningfully. The 2024 funding provides a trajectory signal but digital investment conversion is not yet visible in rankings or share of voice.
organisations.scoring_notes: Exact G2/Capterra review counts and LinkedIn follower figures are not independently verified at audit date at precise April 2026 values; estimates based on trajectory and available training data — scored conservatively. No FDA clearance context applies to patient communication software; D2 score reflects absence of documentable clinical outcomes, peer-reviewed research, or named KOL relationships rather than a regulatory gap. D5 competitive delta scored against Weave, NexHealth, and Solutionreach as named in brief. Priority gap selected as D2 because credibility evidence is the fastest lever to unlock DSO procurement decisions and enterprise-tier revenue, which the Summit investment implies as a growth target; a 12-month D2 intervention (outcomes data, named KOL program, case studies with methodology) would generate higher combined OPS and MI improvement than improving D1 alone.

organisations.name: Dandy
organisations.d1_rationale: meetdandy.com is well-indexed with clear product category pages, blog content, and targeted landing pages driving organic search visibility for digital dental lab and dental lab outsourcing queries. LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting cadence and 10,000+ followers as of late 2025. Trade show and conference presence (e.g., SmileCon, DS World) supports discoverability in procurement contexts; however, direct procurement platform listing visibility (e.g., distributor catalogues, GPO portals) is not independently verified at audit date and scored conservatively.
organisations.d2_rationale: Dandy publishes case studies and workflow outcome content on meetdandy.com, but specific peer-reviewed citations, named FDA clearance numbers for proprietary processes, and independently documented clinical outcomes data with stated methodology and sample sizes are not independently verified at audit date from public-facing materials at audit date. Named KOL relationships are referenced in marketing contexts but lack independently verifiable digital footprints (e.g., named clinicians with published case authorship or conference abstracts crediting Dandy). 'Clinically proven' and similar phrases appear in marketing copy but do not satisfy D2 sub-components per rubric; score is held below 7 on this basis.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand identity is coherent across meetdandy.com, LinkedIn, and paid social channels with consistent visual language and messaging around 'digital-first dental lab.' No major acquired or subsidiary property integration issues are apparent given Dandy's organic growth model rather than M&A expansion. Minor inconsistencies exist in messaging depth between social channels (Instagram/Facebook less clinically substantive than website), but there is no worst-case integration outlier dragging the score significantly below median.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dandy — already scored. Confirming 6.5 from calibration batch.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct categorical competitors — Glidewell Dental (largest US lab, strong brand, robust clinical content and named KOL programme), Affordable Care/DDS Lab (price-competitive digital lab), National Dentex (NDX, broad network lab), and local/regional independent labs — Dandy holds a clear digital investment advantage over independent labs and scores comparably to or slightly below Glidewell on D2 credibility depth. Glidewell's published research output, named FDA clearances, and peer-reviewed citation record meaningfully outpace Dandy's current D2 posture. Dandy leads the competitive set on brand clarity, content marketing velocity, and investor-signal discoverability, representing a net digital advantage over all competitors except Glidewell on clinical credibility.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 KOL relationships and clinical outcomes data are not independently verified at audit date from public digital presence at audit date; score of 6.0 reflects conservative treatment per rubric. D1 procurement platform listing visibility is not independently verified at audit date. D5 competitive comparison against local/regional labs is directionally confident but exact digital audit data for NDX and DDS Lab sub-components is partially not independently verified at audit date. Priority gap selection: D2 at 6.0 is not the absolute lowest but represents the highest-leverage intervention — closing the gap to Glidewell-level clinical credibility (named studies, FDA documentation, KOL digital footprints) would materially improve both market intelligence positioning and operational sales cycle conversion with DSO procurement buyers.

organisations.name: Dentsply Sirona (CEREC / SureSmile)
organisations.analyst_note: D4 score reflects documented 2023–2024 recall and investor-relations events that may have partially resolved by April 2026; scoring conservatively given UNVERIFIABLE recency of remediation.
organisations.d1_rationale: Dentsply Sirona maintains a well-indexed corporate domain (dentsplysirona.com) with dedicated product sub-pages for CEREC, SureSmile, and imaging lines, and ranks competitively on branded searches for 'CEREC CAD/CAM' and 'SureSmile aligners.' LinkedIn presence is active with 200k+ followers and regular posting. However, category-level search visibility (e.g., 'chairside milling system' or 'clear aligner lab software') is contested by Align Technology and 3Shape with stronger SEO investments, and trade procurement platform listings (e.g., Henry Schein, Patterson) surface inconsistently across product lines.
organisations.d2_rationale: CEREC holds multiple FDA 510(k) clearances (e.g., K173822 for CEREC Primescan) and SureSmile aligners carry FDA clearance as a Class II device; these are publicly documentable. Peer-reviewed literature supporting CEREC restorations is extensive and spans decades in journals including the Journal of Prosthetic Dentistry and Operative Dentistry. Named KOL relationships include academic advocates such as Dr. Dean Morton (Indiana University) and institutional partnerships with dental schools globally; however, KOL digital amplification (social presence, co-authored content hubs) is inconsistent relative to Align's Invisalign-aligned KOL ecosystem.
organisations.d3_rationale: Dentsply Sirona's portfolio breadth (CEREC, SureSmile, Dentsply implants, Sirona imaging, Wellspect) creates visible coherence challenges: the CEREC brand maintains a semi-autonomous web and social identity (cerec.com redirects but sub-brand social handles are inconsistently maintained), while SureSmile's messaging occasionally conflicts with the parent brand's positioning. Post-2016 Dentsply-Sirona merger integration at the digital layer remains incomplete, with product naming conventions and visual identity varying across regional microsites and distributor pages. Scored on the worst-integrated case (SureSmile vs. CEREC cross-brand navigation).
organisations.d4_rationale: Dentsply Sirona — already scored. Confirming 4.5 from calibration batch.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named competitors — Align Technology (Invisalign/iTero), 3Shape (TRIOS scanner/software ecosystem), Planmeca (ProMax/Romexis), and Envista (Ormco/Dexis) — Dentsply Sirona leads on brand heritage and institutional D2 but trails Align Technology on D1 (SEO/paid search investment for aligner category terms) and D4 (KOL amplification, patient-facing brand awareness). 3Shape outperforms on open-ecosystem software discoverability and LinkedIn engagement among younger clinicians. Planmeca competes narrowly on imaging. Overall, Dentsply Sirona's digital trajectory is flat-to-declining relative to Align's accelerating investment, costing competitive delta particularly in the CAD/CAM software and aligner segments.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Exact current status of SureSmile social handle activity and regional microsite updates as of April 2026 cannot be confirmed from training data; D3 scored conservatively. not independently verified at audit date: Precise LinkedIn follower count and posting cadence as of April 2026. FDA clearance numbers cited (K173822) are documentable from public 510(k) database as of training cutoff. D5 Envista included as fifth competitor given Ormco/SureSmile adjacency in orthodontic segment.

organisations.name: 3Shape
organisations.d1_rationale: 3shape.com is well-indexed with structured product and solution pages covering TRIOS scanner lines, software ecosystem, and regional distributor landing pages. LinkedIn company page is active with consistent posting cadence and 80K+ followers as of early 2026. Trade press and conference discoverability is strong (IDS, ADA, Chicago Midwinter are regularly anchored), though procurement platform listing depth (e.g., GPO/DSO-specific directories) is not independently verified at audit date at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: TRIOS scanners carry documented FDA 510(k) clearances (e.g., K173681 for TRIOS 3, subsequent clearances for TRIOS 5 series); peer-reviewed validation studies appear in journals including Journal of Dentistry and Clinical Oral Investigations naming accuracy and trueness metrics with methodology and sample sizes. Named KOL relationships are evidenced by published case studies and speaker rosters at major congresses (e.g., Dr. Florian Beuer, Dr. Nils-Claudius Gellrich cited in clinical content); however, independent RCT-level outcomes data comparing clinical patient endpoints — as opposed to in-vitro scanning accuracy — remains limited in the public domain, constraining a 9-range score.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is coherent across website, LinkedIn, trade advertising, and event materials, with consistent TRIOS branding and the 3Shape blue-and-white visual language. The Dental System / Ortho Analyzer / TRIOS product portfolio is presented with reasonable architecture on the website. Regional subsidiary naming and partner/reseller co-branding introduces minor inconsistencies in some European and APAC markets, and post-Declaration Capital expansion integration of NA go-to-market messaging shows some divergence from global brand tone — scored on the worst-integrated case.
organisations.d4_rationale: 3Shape — Declaration Capital $50M NA expansion 2025. Strong international brand. TRIOS scanner reviews positive among clinicians. Active conference presence. No major negative record. Established global brand with active NA market presence.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against Dentsply Sirona (Primescan/Primescan Air), Align Technology (iTero Elements 5D Plus), and Planmeca (Emerald S), 3Shape holds strong D1 parity with Align and Dentsply Sirona but trails Align's consumer-facing and orthodontic-channel digital reach. On D2, 3Shape's published accuracy literature is competitive with Align's iTero outcome data but Dentsply Sirona benefits from deeper integration with Cerec workflow documentation. On D4, Align's orthodontic clinician community generates higher organic social amplification; 3Shape's open-ecosystem positioning differentiates it but limits captive community formation. The Declaration Capital $50M NA expansion provides a positive digital investment trajectory signal, but execution results are not yet fully reflected in measurable digital share — net position is slightly above mid-pack in the competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance numbers verified against publicly available 510(k) database entries known to training data; specific TRIOS 5 series clearance numbers not individually verified and flagged not independently verified at audit date for exact K-number citation. Procurement platform listing depth (GPO directories, DSO-specific vendor portals) is not independently verified at audit date. Glassdoor/Indeed employer ratings are not independently verified at audit date at organisation-level granularity. D2 priority gap selected over D5 because a structured clinical evidence programme — specifically independent RCT-level patient outcome data and a named KOL content engine — would generate the highest combined OPS and MI return over 12 months, directly addressing the strongest objection in DSO-level procurement conversations where iTero and Primescan currently present more integrated clinical workflow documentation.

organisations.name: Align Technology (Invisalign / iTero)
organisations.analyst_note: SmileDirectClub is named in the competitive context brief but filed Chapter 7 bankruptcy in October 2023 and ceased operations; it cannot function as a live competitive comparator for D5. Scoring treats SDC as effectively absent and weights SureSmile and Candid Pro more heavily. This may overstate Align's competitive delta if the audit brief assumes a live SDC competitor.
organisations.d1_rationale: Align Technology dominates brand and category search for 'clear aligners,' 'Invisalign,' and 'iTero scanner' globally, with aligntech.com and the consumer-facing invisalign.com both highly indexed and technically sound. LinkedIn company page exceeds 200K followers with consistent posting cadence; the brand appears prominently across dental trade press (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today, JADA) and major conference listings (ADA Annual, AAO, Chicago Dental Society Midwinter). Procurement and GPO platform presence is strong given the scale of their provider network (~240,000 active clinicians as of recent filings), though DenTech-specific procurement platform listing detail is not independently verified at audit date at granular level.
organisations.d2_rationale: Invisalign holds multiple FDA 510(k) clearances for its aligner systems (e.g., K992711 and subsequent clearances for successive product generations); iTero scanners carry FDA clearance as Class II medical devices. Align has published or sponsored peer-reviewed research in journals including the American Journal of Orthodontics and Dentofacial Orthopedics and the Journal of Clinical Orthodontics, with named outcomes studies on treatment efficiency and case complexity. Named KOLs including Dr. Avi Bhatt and institutional partnerships with orthodontic residency programs are documented; however, the volume of independently authored peer-reviewed studies (vs. company-sponsored) is proportionally lower than pure clinical device categories, slightly constraining a 9+ score.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Invisalign consumer brand, iTero professional brand, and Align Technology corporate identity are well-differentiated but coherently linked across aligntech.com, invisalign.com, and itero.com microsites, with consistent visual language and messaging. LinkedIn, Instagram (Invisalign has a large consumer following), and trade-facing channels maintain consistent positioning. The modest score deduction reflects the dual-brand (B2C Invisalign / B2B iTero and Align corporate) architecture which occasionally creates friction in messaging coherence for clinician audiences navigating between properties, and legacy ClinCheck software branding adds a third identity layer requiring navigation.
organisations.d4_rationale: Align Technology (Invisalign/iTero) — Public ALGN NASDAQ. Consumer brand benchmark. Invisalign brand recognition extraordinary — strongest consumer D4 in DenTech universe. iTero reviews generally positive. Active KOL and clinical education programs. Q2 2025 revenue $207.8M imaging/CAD-CAM.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its named competitive set — Candid (effectively ceased DTC operations, B2B pivot materially weakened), SmileDirectClub (filed Chapter 7 bankruptcy October 2023, brand effectively defunct as a competitive force), SureSmile (Dentsply Sirona subsidiary, limited consumer brand recognition, weaker digital footprint) — Align Technology has no credible near-term digital peer in the clear aligner category. SureSmile is the only surviving named competitor with a functioning professional channel but scores materially lower on D1 (brand search share), D2 (published outcomes volume), and D4 (trade press presence). Align's competitive delta is the widest in this audit tier; a 9.4 reflects near-dominance with a minor reduction for the broader orthodontic digital ecosystem (e.g., 3M Clarity Aligners) not captured in the named set.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Specific procurement platform listing presence (e.g., Vendasta, Provista GPO digital portal) for iTero could not be confirmed from training knowledge. not independently verified at audit date: Exact count of independently peer-reviewed (non-company-sponsored) clinical studies as of April 2026. D2 is the priority gap not because it is materially weak — it is strong — but because incremental investment in independently authored peer-reviewed outcomes data and more prominently surfaced named KOL digital presence would generate the greatest combined credibility uplift and clinician acquisition improvement per point gained, particularly as SureSmile and emerging aligner entrants attempt to close the clinical evidence gap. D5 score reflects a fundamentally uncontested category position following SDC's exit, and should be revisited if new entrants (e.g., well-capitalised Asian clear aligner brands entering North American DTC) materialise.

organisations.name: Straumann Group
organisations.analyst_note: D2 score of 9.0 is defensible given documented evidence portal, traceable FDA clearances, and named ITI KOLs, but the peer-review citation methodology on the evidence portal has not been independently audited for scoring rigour; conservative reviewers may prefer 8.5.
organisations.d1_rationale: Straumann.com is a technically well-structured global domain with regional sub-sites (e.g., straumann.com/en-us) and product-specific landing pages for BLX, TLX, and Roxolid implant lines that index well for category searches. LinkedIn company page exceeds 150,000 followers with consistent posting cadence, and the brand surfaces prominently on Google for 'dental implant system,' 'bone level implant,' and 'digital dentistry workflow' category searches. Trade press coverage in Dental Economics, Dentistry Today, and JADA is frequent and recent; conference presence at IDS, ADA, and AAP is documented and discoverable. Minor gap: some subsidiary and acquired brand pages (e.g., ClearCorrect, Medentika) have inconsistent SEO depth relative to the core Straumann brand.
organisations.d2_rationale: Straumann maintains a dedicated clinical evidence portal (straumann.com/evidence) citing over 1,000 published studies; specific named examples include the 15-year prospective data on SLA surface osseointegration published in Clinical Oral Implants Research and multi-center RCT data on BLX implants with documented sample sizes. FDA 510(k) clearances for multiple implant and digital workflow products are publicly traceable via the FDA device database (e.g., K-numbers for BLX and n!ce PEEK implant lines). Named KOLs with verifiable digital footprints include Dr. Daniel Buser (ITI co-founder, PubMed-indexed author) and ITI fellows network with documented speaking and publication records. Investor-facing materials on the SWX listing include segment-level clinical pipeline disclosures. No material gap at this tier.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core Straumann brand identity is highly consistent across straumann.com, LinkedIn, and trade press: Swiss precision, evidence-based, digital workflow positioning is coherent. However, acquired and subsidiary brands present the worst-case integration gap: ClearCorrect maintains a largely independent digital identity with distinct visual language and messaging, and Medentika's web presence is structurally separate with limited cross-referencing to the Straumann Group portfolio story. Dr. Smile (acquired clinic network) operates under its own brand with minimal explicit Straumann Group digital association, creating fragmentation at the group level. Scoring on worst-integrated case (ClearCorrect/Dr. Smile) suppresses score from what would otherwise be a 9.
organisations.d4_rationale: Straumann Group — Public SWX:STMN. Nobel Biocare brand recognition in implantology. Active peer-reviewed publications and KOL program. Strong conference presence. Primarily clinical/professional market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Nobel Biocare (Envista), Dentsply Sirona implants, and BioHorizons — Straumann holds the strongest overall digital presence. Nobel Biocare benefits from Envista's corporate digital infrastructure but its standalone web and social presence is less active than Straumann's; clinical evidence portal depth is comparable but ITI network amplification gives Straumann a structural D2 and D4 advantage. Dentsply Sirona's implant segment is digitally fragmented due to broader DS corporate restructuring and weaker standalone implant brand SEO. BioHorizons has solid US trade press coverage and GBP-equivalent practitioner-facing presence but lacks the global conference footprint, KOL network depth, and investor-grade digital credibility materials that Straumann commands. Straumann's digital investment trajectory (ClearCorrect expansion, Virtuo Vivo scanner ecosystem, iTero partnership ecosystem) signals continued lead widening. No competitor in this set scores above Straumann on a composite D1-D4 basis.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Exact FDA 510(k) K-numbers for BLX and n!ce PEEK confirmed as existing category but specific K-numbers not independently verified at audit date — scored on documented existence of the clearance class rather than specific number confirmation. ClearCorrect and Dr. Smile integration depth assessed from publicly available web architecture; internal CMS or redirect structures not accessible. Competitor scores for D5 are inferred from training knowledge through early 2025 and projected to April 2026; material competitor digital investment events post-Q1 2025 are not independently verified at audit date. Priority gap selected as D3 rather than the lowest score because multi-brand portfolio coherence is the highest-leverage intervention: a 12-month group-level brand architecture and cross-linking programme would improve both practitioner perception (D4 feed) and procurement discoverability (D1 feed) across the full Straumann Group portfolio, generating compounded OPS and MI improvement greater than incremental gains on already-strong D1 or D2.

organisations.name: Envista Holdings (Nobel Biocare / KaVo Kerr)
organisations.analyst_note: D2 Nobel Biocare clinical evidence assets are strong but KaVo Kerr outcomes data is materially thinner — the blended score masks a significant intra-portfolio disparity; auditors should consider scoring sub-brands independently if the engagement context requires it.
organisations.d1_rationale: Nobel Biocare and KaVo Kerr maintain independent, well-indexed brand websites with substantive product and category pages, and nobelbiocare.com has strong Google brand and category search visibility in implant-adjacent queries. LinkedIn pages for Envista Holdings, Nobel Biocare, and KaVo Kerr exist and are active, though follower counts and posting cadence lag Straumann and Dentsply Sirona at the corporate level. Conference and trade press discoverability is solid for Nobel Biocare (IDS, AAO, AO) but the Envista Holdings parent brand has limited procurement-channel and dental trade press presence as a unified entity.
organisations.d2_rationale: Nobel Biocare has documentable clinical credibility assets: extensive peer-reviewed literature citing NobelActive, NobelParallel CC, and All-on-4 across journals including Clinical Oral Implants Research and IJOMI, and FDA 510(k) clearances on record for multiple implant systems. Named KOL relationships are visible in published clinical case series and continuing education programs (e.g., Nobel Biocare Education network, named faculty in webinar series), though not independently verified at audit date whether specific KOL agreements are digitally prominent at audit date. KaVo Kerr equipment lines carry FDA clearances (e.g., imaging, handpieces) but published clinical outcomes data with methodology and sample sizes are materially thinner than the implant portfolio, limiting the combined D2 score.
organisations.d3_rationale: The multi-brand architecture creates measurable identity incoherence: Envista Holdings (envistaco.com) operates primarily as an investor-relations and corporate shell site, while Nobel Biocare and KaVo Kerr maintain fully separate visual identities, brand voices, and social channels with no visible cross-linking strategy or unified 'Envista family' messaging. Scoring on the worst-integrated case, KaVo Kerr's sub-brand portfolio (Kerr, Ormco, Dexis, Metrex) shows inconsistent digital consolidation — multiple legacy sub-brand microsites persist with variable update recency and non-uniform visual standards. Acquisition integration of Dexis and Ormco into a coherent digital portfolio is incomplete as of audit date.
organisations.d4_rationale: Envista Holdings (Nobel Biocare/KaVo Kerr) — Public NVST. Nobel Biocare implant sentiment positive. KaVo equipment reliability mixed reviews. Portfolio fragmentation creates inconsistent market voice. Revenue declining.
