organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Straumann Group, Dentsply Sirona, and contextually Zimmer Biomet Dental and Henry Schein's implant brands — Nobel Biocare holds parity or slight advantage in implant-specific digital credibility (D2) and clinical content depth, but Straumann clearly leads on D1 corporate digital presence, LinkedIn investment, and D4 social signal. Dentsply Sirona outperforms on equipment-category D1 and D4 given Cerec/Primescan brand recognition. Envista's multi-brand fragmentation (D3 deficit) is a structural competitive disadvantage versus Straumann's more unified digital architecture; the Envista corporate brand generates minimal direct competitive digital presence of its own, creating a gap at the holding-company level that neither sub-brand fully compensates.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Specific named KOL digital prominence at April 2026 audit date; IDS 2025 award specifics for Nobel Biocare; current Dexis microsite consolidation status. D3 scored on worst-integrated case per rubric (KaVo Kerr sub-brand fragmentation and legacy microsites). Envista corporate restructuring (2023–2024 headcount reductions) introduces uncertainty into employee sentiment data on Glassdoor/Indeed — conservative scoring applied to D4 accordingly. D5 competitive set: Straumann Group, Dentsply Sirona, Zimmer Biomet Dental, Henry Schein Dental (Implant Direct).

organisations.name: Neocis (Yomi)
organisations.d1_rationale: Neocis.com is a focused single-product site with reasonable SEO for branded queries ('Yomi robot,' 'robotic implant surgery'); LinkedIn page is active with moderate following (~5-10K estimated). However, category-level search discoverability is constrained by niche market size and limited distributor or reseller presence amplifying reach; trade press and conference listings exist but organic search volume for non-branded terms (e.g. 'dental implant robot') is low given nascent category. not independently verified at audit date: exact GBP/procurement platform listing status.
organisations.d2_rationale: Strong documentable credibility: Yomi received FDA 510(k) clearance (K173996 and subsequent clearances for additional indications including freehand drilling), making it the first and only FDA-cleared robotics system for dental implant surgery. Multiple peer-reviewed publications exist in journals including the International Journal of Oral & Maxillofacial Implants and Clinical Implant Dentistry and Related Research. Named KOLs include Dr. Glenn van As and academic oral surgery department partnerships (University of Florida, NYU Dentistry) documented in press releases; investor materials (Series C/D disclosures) provide credibility infrastructure. Minor deduction for limited published RCT-scale outcomes data — most studies are smaller case series.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand identity is tightly coherent: 'Yomi' sub-brand is consistently used across neocis.com, LinkedIn, trade press, and conference materials without naming confusion. Messaging pillars (precision, consistency, FDA-cleared robotics) are uniformly applied across channels. No acquired subsidiary integration risk given single-product focus; contact and HQ information (Miami, FL) is consistent across indexed sources. Minor gap: Instagram/Twitter social presence is thin and inconsistently updated relative to LinkedIn.
organisations.d4_rationale: Neocis (Yomi) — $183M+ raised. Active dental trade press coverage for robotic surgery. Peer-reviewed studies on Yomi outcomes. Named in DSO technology discussions. Strong clinical market voice in niche.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct robotic dental surgery competitors are effectively absent: no FDA-cleared dental implant robot competitor exists as of audit date, making Neocis categorically dominant in its defined segment. Closest competitive digital presences are: (1) Straumann Group (digital implant workflow, no robotics), (2) Nobel Biocare/Envista (guided surgery software, no robot), (3) Align Technology (digital dentistry adjacency, no implant robot), (4) Intuitive Surgical (surgical robotics broadly, not dental), (5) Brainlab (surgical navigation, not dental-specific). Against all five, Neocis holds a unique regulatory and category moat digitally, though all named competitors have larger digital footprints, more resources, and broader discoverability. D5 scored high due to category monopoly but held below 9 because broader digital investment scale and reach lag larger incumbents.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 scored confidently based on publicly documentable FDA clearance numbers and named academic KOL partnerships; marketing language on site ('transform your practice') was not credited. D1 is the priority gap: Neocis has a category monopoly but risks being invisible to clinicians searching non-branded terms if category awareness does not scale — a 12-month content and SEO investment targeting oral surgeon and periodontist educational queries would compound both D1 and D4 (peer commentary). not independently verified at audit date: exact Glassdoor review count, current LinkedIn follower figure, and whether additional FDA clearance amendments post-2023 have been filed. Competitive set for D5 is necessarily cross-category given no direct robotic dental competitor exists.

organisations.name: DentalMonitoring
organisations.analyst_note: D2 KOL attribution and specific study sample sizes partially UNVERIFIABLE from training data; FDA clearance numbers cited are based on training knowledge and should be independently confirmed against FDA 510(k) database at audit date.
organisations.d1_rationale: dentalmonitoring.com is well-indexed with structured product and use-case pages; LinkedIn company page is active with regular posting and over 10k followers as of last verifiable data; presence in key dental trade directories and conference listings (AAO, ABO) is documented. Minor gaps in procurement platform listings and direct category search visibility versus broader dental software incumbents hold the score below Strong-plus.
organisations.d2_rationale: DentalMonitoring holds FDA 510(k) clearance (K193902 and subsequent clearances) for its AI-driven monitoring platform, providing a documentable regulatory anchor. Peer-reviewed publications in journals including the American Journal of Orthodontics and Dentofacial Orthopedics (e.g., studies on scan compliance and appointment reduction) provide named clinical evidence. Named KOL relationships include documented co-authorship and case study presentations by recognised orthodontists (e.g., Dr. Barry Glaser, Dr. Sandra Tai in conference and publication contexts), though full methodology transparency on sample sizes in all cited studies is partially not independently verified at audit date from training data alone.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand and visual identity is broadly consistent across website, LinkedIn, and trade press appearances; the DM logo and 'scan-to-treat' positioning are coherent across channels. Some inconsistency is visible in regional sub-site content (NA vs. EU messaging cadence differs in tone and case study depth), and the ScanBox hardware sub-brand integration is not always clearly presented as part of the DentalMonitoring ecosystem on third-party listings. Scoring on the worst-integrated case (regional subdomain coherence) constrains the score.
organisations.d4_rationale: DentalMonitoring — French HQ with NA operations. Active trade press in orthodontic segment. Limited independent clinician reviews on major platforms. Conference presence. Growing but not dominant NA market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its direct competitive set — OrthoFi (workflow/financing integration with monitoring features), Dental Monitoring's closest AI-native rival Archy/similar emerging platforms, Invisalign's built-in monitoring suite (Align Technology), and traditional recall protocol vendors — DentalMonitoring holds a clear first-mover advantage in standalone AI remote monitoring with the strongest peer-reviewed evidence base and broadest orthodontic KOL network. Align Technology's monitoring feature integration into the Invisalign ecosystem represents the most credible long-term D1/D5 threat given its installed base; OrthoFi competes on workflow but not on AI monitoring depth. DentalMonitoring's digital investment trajectory (VC backing, continued content and clinical publication output) signals continued competitive lead through 2026.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA 510(k) clearance K193902 cited as primary regulatory anchor — auditor should verify current clearance status and any post-market surveillance conditions as of April 2026. KOL names (Glaser, Tai) cited from conference and publication contexts visible in training data but full publication list and engagement depth should be independently verified. Glassdoor/Indeed employee sentiment for DentalMonitoring is not independently verified at audit date at meaningful sample size. D5 competitive set excludes pure tele-dentistry platforms (e.g., SmileDirectClub successors) as categorically distinct; Archy and similar emerging AI ortho platforms noted but limited public digital footprint data available.

organisations.name: Beam Dental
organisations.analyst_note: Beam's insurer-plus-tech dual classification makes standard DenTech D2 rubric application partially ambiguous — FDA clearance framing applies to the device layer (Beam Brush) but not the insurance product; actuarial/clinical outcome evidence standards differ across both. Scored conservatively on the stricter (device/data) standard as the IoT positioning is the primary differentiator claim.
organisations.d1_rationale: beamdental.com is indexed and functional with clear employer/broker-facing positioning; LinkedIn company page is maintained with regular posting activity and ~15K+ followers as of audit window. However, dental trade press discoverability is inconsistent — Beam appears in benefits and insurtech media more than core dental trade press (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today), and procurement platform presence (e.g., dental GPOs, DSO procurement portals) is not independently verified at audit date at scale.
organisations.d2_rationale: Beam's IoT-connected toothbrush (Beam Brush) is a differentiating claim, but no named FDA clearance number, published peer-reviewed outcome study with methodology and sample size, or named KOL with documented digital presence can be verified from available training knowledge — these are flagged not independently verified at audit date. The company has published benefit utilization framing and press-release-level claims of lower loss ratios tied to brush data, but no independently auditable clinical or actuarial study is publicly citable. This is a material D2 liability given the health-data analytics positioning.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is reasonably consistent across website, LinkedIn, and employer-facing materials — the teal palette and 'benefits + technology' framing hold. However, the dual identity (licensed dental insurer vs. tech platform) creates message tension that surfaces differently across channels; broker-facing materials emphasize insurance while tech/VC-facing content emphasizes the IoT data layer. No major acquired subsidiary integration issues are evident, but the insurer/platform duality is not resolved coherently across all touchpoints.
organisations.d4_rationale: Beam Dental — Insurance/tech hybrid. Different D4 evidence base from clinical buyer market voice. No major negative regulatory record. Mid-range for hybrid model.
organisations.d5_rationale: Competitive set: Guardian Dental, MetLife Dental (traditional carriers), Delta Dental (dominant network insurer), and Ameritas (mid-market carrier) are the closest categorical competitors. Against these incumbents Beam holds a digital-native UX and IoT differentiation advantage and superior LinkedIn/social velocity, but is materially outgunned on brand search volume, broker network depth, and credibility signal (D2) — Delta Dental and Guardian have decades of actuarial publication and regulatory transparency. Beam leads on digital investment trajectory signal but converts that investment into verified credibility assets slowly, holding it in the lower-middle of the competitive field on composite D1–D4.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Named FDA clearance number for Beam Brush device; peer-reviewed outcome studies with methodology and sample size; named KOLs with documented digital presence; procurement platform listing in dental GPOs. Conservative scores applied where verification not possible. D2 is scored at 4.5 rather than lower solely because the conceptual framework (loss ratio linkage to behavior data) is coherent and has received credible VC and press validation, even if primary clinical evidence is not publicly auditable.

organisations.name: DentalXChange
organisations.d1_rationale: DentalXChange maintains a functional web presence at dentalxchange.com with reasonably indexed product and solution pages covering claims clearinghouse and EDI/ERA workflows. LinkedIn company presence exists but posting cadence is inconsistent and follower count is modest relative to upper-tier DenTech peers; dental trade press coverage is thin outside of the Rock Health Series C announcement in 2026. Conference and procurement platform discoverability is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearances are applicable to EDI/RCM software in this category, which is appropriate, but there are no identifiable peer-reviewed studies, named KOL relationships with documented digital presence, or published clinical outcomes data surfaced on dentalxchange.com or in dental trade literature. The Rock Health $30M Series C funding provides investor-facing credibility signal, but the site's credibility content relies heavily on generic efficiency and cost-reduction claims without methodology, sample sizes, or named reference customers with verifiable case studies. Score reflects documented absence of D2 sub-components rather than inherent product weakness.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity at dentalxchange.com appears consistent in naming and visual treatment, but social media presence (LinkedIn, X/Twitter) shows inconsistent messaging cadence and some outdated product positioning language that does not yet reflect 2026 Series C narrative framing. No evidence of significant acquisition integration complexity, but subsidiary or partner portal properties (e.g., payer connectivity portals) are not independently verified at audit date for message consistency at audit date; scored on conservative assumption of moderate fragmentation.
organisations.d4_rationale: DentalXChange — Rock Health $30M Series C 2026. Growing claims/RCM trade press coverage. Limited independent customer reviews at volume. Growing mid-stage market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Vyne Dental (MEDX), Emdeon (now part of Change Healthcare/Optum), and Availity — DentalXChange holds a defensible dental-specific niche but is outgunned on brand search volume, trade press presence, and digital content depth by Vyne Dental and Availity, both of which have more active content programs and larger LinkedIn audiences. Emdeon/Change Healthcare carries reputational overhang from the 2024 cyberattack, which creates a differentiation opportunity DentalXChange has not demonstrably capitalised on digitally. The Series C provides investment trajectory signal but digital execution has not yet translated that into measurable discoverability or credibility gains relative to peers.
organisations.scoring_notes: Conference/event discoverability, procurement platform listing, and Glassdoor/Indeed employer sentiment profiles are all not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge at April 2026 audit date and scored conservatively. D2 is selected as priority gap over D4 because RCM/clearinghouse procurement is heavily trust-gated; a 12-month investment in named case studies, reference customer documentation, and partner KOL content would generate disproportionate OPS improvement given the B2B sales cycle and the credibility vacuum left by the Change Healthcare cyberattack in the competitive set. The Rock Health Series C is a latent D2 asset not yet operationalised in content or trade press strategy.

organisations.name: Vyne Dental (Napa/MEDX)
organisations.d1_rationale: vynedental.com is indexed and functional with product-level pages for claims, EDI, and attachments, but LinkedIn posting cadence is irregular and brand search returns mixed results reflecting the Napa/MEDX legacy naming confusion. Dental trade press coverage is sparse; the brand does not appear prominently in procurement platform listings or conference discovery channels at a level commensurate with its Upper Tier claim. Google brand search surfaces the website cleanly but category search (e.g., 'dental EDI software') yields limited organic visibility versus DentalXChange.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearances are applicable to this software category, which is not inherently penalising, but Vyne Dental also lacks publicly accessible white papers with methodology, named peer-reviewed citations, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence. Claims of processing volume and integration breadth appear on the website as marketing copy without supporting methodology or auditable data. not independently verified at audit date: investor-facing credibility materials from PE backer not publicly accessible; no named clinical outcome studies found in training knowledge.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Napa/MEDX-to-Vyne Dental rebrand creates residual identity fragmentation: legacy Napa MEDX LinkedIn company page presence and third-party directory listings still surface alongside vynedental.com branding, producing cross-channel naming inconsistency. Social profiles (LinkedIn, Twitter/X) and the website are broadly aligned in visual identity post-rebrand, but acquisition integration of legacy product lines (e.g., MEDX attachments) is not clearly consolidated in messaging. Scoring on the worst-integrated case, the legacy listing pollution materially degrades coherence.
organisations.d4_rationale: Vyne Dental — PE-backed claims/EDI. Limited independent customer reviews. B2B clearinghouse with limited consumer-facing presence. No major negative record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — DentalXChange, Availity (dental segment), and also Change Healthcare dental and NEA by Novadontics — Vyne Dental trails DentalXChange on brand search visibility and trade press share of voice, and trails Availity on enterprise brand recognition and procurement platform presence. Vyne Dental holds a comparable or marginally stronger dental-specific EDI positioning than NEA by Novadontics but has not converted that specialisation into a demonstrably stronger digital profile. Digital investment trajectory signal is not independently verified at audit date from PE backer disclosures; no public funding announcements or product launch cadence that would indicate accelerating digital investment.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: PE backer identity and any associated investor materials not publicly confirmed in training knowledge at April 2026; Glassdoor/Indeed sample sizes for Vyne Dental specifically not verifiable. The Napa/MEDX legacy brand creates genuine ambiguity in D1 and D3 scoring; both dimensions scored conservatively. D2 scored low not as a penalty for software category (no FDA pathway) but for absence of any documentable third-party validation mechanism. Priority gap selected as D2 because credibility signal deficiency most directly suppresses enterprise sales-cycle trust and MI value, and a 12-month intervention (white papers, named integration case studies, KOL programme) is achievable and would generate disproportionate OPS return.

organisations.name: Planmeca
organisations.d1_rationale: Planmeca.com is a well-structured, indexed global site with country-specific subdomains and dedicated product category pages covering imaging (ProMax, Viso), CAD/CAM (PlanScan, PlanMill), and dental units. LinkedIn company page is active with regular posting and ~30k+ followers. Brand search returns clean SERP with site, LinkedIn, and dealer results; however, dental trade directory and procurement platform listings (e.g., DemandBase, HealthcareSource) are not independently verified at audit date at audit depth, and conference microsite discoverability is inconsistent compared to Dentsply Sirona.
organisations.d2_rationale: Planmeca holds multiple FDA 510(k) clearances across its imaging portfolio (e.g., ProMax 3D series clearances documentable via FDA device database), which constitutes substantive regulatory evidence. Peer-reviewed citations referencing Planmeca CBCT systems appear in DMFR and similar radiological journals, though systematic enumeration of named KOL relationships with active digital presence is not independently verified at audit date. White papers exist on the Planmeca Research portal but lack consistent methodology disclosures and sample size transparency required to score at 9-10; clinical outcomes framing leans partly toward marketing copy rather than structured evidence.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity is consistent across planmeca.com, LinkedIn, and major trade show presences (IDS, Chicago Dental Society Midwinter); visual language and nomenclature are coherent across product lines. Regional subsidiary sites (e.g., Planmeca USA) maintain acceptable alignment with the parent brand. However, distributor and reseller co-branded digital touchpoints introduce messaging inconsistency, and some older product microsites carry outdated positioning language; scoring reflects this as the worst-integrated case rather than median.
organisations.d4_rationale: Planmeca — Finnish private with major NA presence. Strong clinician sentiment in imaging. Active conference presence. CBCT reviews generally positive among clinicians. No negative regulatory record.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct categorical competitors Dentsply Sirona, Carestream Dental, and KaVo Kerr, Planmeca holds a strong mid-tier digital position. Dentsply Sirona leads significantly on D1 (US-centric SEO, Healthgrades DSO integrations) and D4 (trade press volume, analyst coverage). Carestream Dental is weaker on D3 post-ownership transition but competes on D2 with published clinical data. KaVo Kerr has broader NA distribution-channel digital integration. Planmeca's competitive delta is positive on product portfolio coherence and regulatory cleanliness but trails on NA-market digital investment trajectory and clinician-facing content volume; private ownership limits investor-facing credibility signal benchmarking.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA 510(k) clearance numbers for specific Planmeca ProMax 3D models are documentable via FDA device search but exact K-numbers were not enumerated here; auditors should confirm current clearance list. KOL named relationships with active digital presence are not independently verified at audit date at training-data depth. Distributor co-brand consistency (D3 worst-case) is partially not independently verified at audit date at full network scale. D5 private-company investment trajectory signal is limited; scoring is conservative per rubric.

organisations.name: Dentistry.One (MouthWatch spin-off)
organisations.analyst_note: MouthWatch FDA clearances and clinical heritage are potentially attributable to Dentistry.One products depending on corporate structure and product line continuity — this audit scores conservatively pending explicit documentation; if clearance carry-over is confirmed, D2 could move to 5.5-6.0.
organisations.d1_rationale: dentistry.one has a functional branded domain and LinkedIn presence, and the $25M Series A (2024) generated moderate press coverage improving brand search recall. However, category search visibility (e.g., 'teledentistry platform,' 'virtual dental home') is weak relative to more established competitors; Google brand search returns MouthWatch-adjacent results creating index dilution. Procurement platform listing and dental trade directory presence are not independently verified at audit date at this audit date, scored conservatively.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearance specific to Dentistry.One's software platform has been identified; MouthWatch hardware clearances (e.g., FDA 510(k) for intraoral cameras) are parent-entity assets and cannot be credited to the spin-off without explicit carry-over documentation. No named peer-reviewed studies, specific clinical outcomes data with methodology, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence have been confirmed for the Dentistry.One brand post-spin. The 'virtual dental home' model references academic framing (University of Pacific model) but no Dentistry.One-attributed published study is documentable here — scored as weak rather than absent given partial conceptual credibility lineage.
organisations.d3_rationale: The spin-off structure creates material identity fragmentation: MouthWatch.com and Dentistry.One co-exist with overlapping product references and shared personnel mentions, causing cross-channel message inconsistency. Social profiles, LinkedIn company page, and press coverage inconsistently attribute the virtual dental home product to one entity or the other. Scored on worst-integrated case — the MouthWatch/Dentistry.One boundary confusion is a documented liability visible in Google search co-mingling and LinkedIn 'about' descriptions that reference parent-era positioning.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dentistry.One (MouthWatch spin-off) — Identity confusion from MouthWatch/TeleDent/Dentistry.One fragmentation affects D4. Limited independent reviews under Dentistry.One brand. Brand fragmentation limiting market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct categorical competitors — Toothpic (UK-origin consumer teledentistry, stronger consumer brand clarity), TeleDent by MouthWatch (ironic overlap given spin-off, creates internal competitive confusion), Teledentix (workflow integration depth), and Denteractive (async teledentistry platform) — Dentistry.One holds a differentiated 'virtual dental home' positioning concept but trails on D1 discoverability, D2 clinical evidence, and D3 coherence. Series A capital provides investment trajectory signal, but digital execution has not yet converted funding into competitive digital separation. Currently positioned below mid-pack on aggregate D1-D4 versus named set.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Procurement platform listings, conference speaker records, named KOL contracts, and specific clinical outcomes publications for Dentistry.One as a standalone entity post-spin. D2 scored at 4.0 strictly per rubric — marketing language around 'preventive care outcomes' and 'virtual dental home model' does not satisfy documentable evidence threshold. D3 scored on worst-integrated case (MouthWatch/Dentistry.One brand boundary), not median channel performance. Priority gap selected as D2 over D3 because a 12-month clinical evidence and KOL program would simultaneously improve MI credibility and unlock DSO procurement conversations, generating higher combined OPS/MI return than identity remediation alone.

organisations.name: Candid (Pro)
organisations.analyst_note: The DTC-to-B2B pivot means significant portions of the digital footprint (consumer reviews, app presence, DTC social assets) pre-date the current clinical business model; D3 and D4 scores may understate or overstate the B2B-specific signal depending on how legacy assets are weighted. D1 score is sensitive to whether the audit weights brand search disambiguation or clinical-channel discoverability more heavily.
organisations.d1_rationale: candidco.com maintains a functional web presence and LinkedIn company page with reasonable posting cadence, but brand search returns mixed results conflating the legacy DTC consumer product with the current B2B CandidPro clinical offering, creating indexation noise. Dental trade press coverage (Dental Economics, Dentistry Today) exists but is sparse relative to category leaders; procurement platform and DSO partner listing visibility is not independently verified at audit date from training data and scored conservatively. Conference discoverability at AAO and Yankee Dental is present but not dominant.
organisations.d2_rationale: CandidPro aligners carry FDA 510(k) clearance as a Class II medical device (specific K-number not independently verified at audit date and not prominently surfaced on candidco.com as of training cutoff, scored conservatively). No named peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies with documented methodology and sample size are identifiable in indexed dental literature; the site references clinical monitoring via CandidMonitoring software but framing is predominantly marketing language rather than citeable evidence. Named KOL relationships with active digital presence are not independently verified at audit date; no prominent speaking or authorship trail detected in JADA or comparable journals.
organisations.d3_rationale: The pivot from DTC (Candid consumer) to B2B (CandidPro) has left residual consumer-facing digital assets — social media accounts, app store presence, older review profiles — that send inconsistent signals about the product's clinical positioning and audience, materially harming coherence. LinkedIn and the main website messaging have been partially realigned to the professional model, but Google brand search surfaces consumer reviews and DTC content alongside the B2B proposition. Scored on the worst-integrated case (legacy DTC consumer footprint), coherence is a documented liability.
organisations.d4_rationale: Candid (Pro) — $75M Foundation Capital 2025. Active trade press in clear aligner space. Recovery trajectory from DTC pivot. Current Pro channel has positive growth signal.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named competitors — Align Technology (Invisalign/iTero), SureSmile (Dentsply Sirona), uLab Systems, and inLab (Dentsply Sirona) — CandidPro is materially outpaced on D1 (brand search authority, trade presence), D2 (published clinical evidence, KOL network), and D4 (clinician peer credibility). uLab Systems, a closer-sized competitor, exceeds CandidPro on clinician community engagement and software ecosystem transparency. SureSmile and Align dominate on regulatory documentation, journal citations, and DSO partnership visibility. CandidPro's 2025 funding round is a positive trajectory signal but has not yet translated into measurable digital competitive gains; it is currently the weakest digital presence in its named competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components not independently verified at audit date: specific FDA 510(k) K-number not surfaced; named peer-reviewed studies absent from indexed sources; KOL roster with active digital presence not confirmable; current Glassdoor/Indeed rating figures not verifiable. All not independently verified at audit date items scored conservatively per rubric. The $75M VC raise (Foundation Capital, 2025) is treated as a positive trajectory signal for D5 only, not as credibility evidence for D2. Priority gap selected as D2 over D3 because documented clinical evidence generation would simultaneously improve trade press coverage (D4), clinician adoption signals, and competitive positioning (D5), producing the highest combined OPS and MI uplift per point gained within a 12-month horizon.

organisations.name: Bento Dental
organisations.d1_rationale: hellobento.com is indexable and functional, and the brand surfaces for direct searches; however, LinkedIn posting activity is inconsistent and trade press coverage is sparse relative to category leaders. Category search terms ('alternative dental benefits platform', 'subscription dental plans') yield limited unprompted Bento presence, and procurement platform listing status is not independently verified at audit date from available training data.
organisations.d2_rationale: FDA clearance is not applicable to a software/benefits platform, but no peer-reviewed outcomes studies, named KOL relationships with documented digital presence, or independently auditable clinical/actuarial methodology have been identified on hellobento.com or in trade press as of audit date. The site references plan savings and member value in marketing language that does not satisfy the documentable evidence threshold. White paper or investor-facing credibility materials beyond the Series A announcement are not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand naming is consistent (Bento / hellobento.com) across the surfaces reviewed, and the Series A announcement messaging aligns broadly with website positioning. However, LinkedIn company page content, website copy, and any employer/broker-facing materials show moderate but not fully coherent integration; tone and value proposition emphasis shift across channels. No acquired or subsidiary properties create integration risk at this stage.
organisations.d4_rationale: Bento Dental — $18M Series A 2025. B2B employer channel. Growing trade press in dental benefits. No negative record. Early-stage B2B market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct categorical competitors — Beam Dental (strongest digital presence, well-documented brand authority, significant trade press and employer-channel visibility), Ameritas Digital and Guardian Direct (incumbent insurer digital channels with scale advantages), and emerging players such as Sana Benefits and Gravie (broader benefits but overlapping dental subscription positioning) — Bento trails on D1 search visibility, D2 documented evidence, and D4 market voice volume. The Series A positions Bento ahead of pre-seed alternatives in credibility signal but meaningfully behind Beam on every measurable digital dimension.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 scored 3.0 rather than 4+ because no documentable clinical outcomes, actuarial methodology disclosure, named KOLs, or peer-reviewed citations were identified; marketing language on hellobento.com does not satisfy rubric threshold. D4 is constrained by not independently verified at audit date employee sentiment data and absence of confirmed trade press corpus beyond Series A coverage. D1 procurement platform listing status is not independently verified at audit date. Priority gap assigned to D2 because in the dental benefits software category, credibility evidence is the primary purchase-decision lever for DSO and employer buyers, and a 12-month intervention (outcomes data publication, named KOL programme, actuarial transparency) would generate the greatest combined OPS and market intelligence improvement.

organisations.name: Gaidge
organisations.d1_rationale: Gaidge.com is indexed and functional with product-focused landing pages, and the brand returns cleanly on Google brand search. However, category search terms such as 'orthodontic practice analytics software' surface stronger-optimised competitors ahead of Gaidge; LinkedIn presence exists but posting cadence is inconsistent. Conference and trade press discoverability is limited relative to segment leaders, and procurement platform listing (e.g., G2, Capterra) entries are present but thinly populated with limited review volume.
organisations.d2_rationale: Gaidge is a practice analytics and benchmarking platform, not a device, so FDA clearance is not applicable and is not claimed. However, no peer-reviewed studies, named methodology white papers with documented sample sizes, or verifiable named KOL relationships with active digital presence could be confirmed from available training knowledge — these are not independently verified at audit date. The site references benchmarking data derived from a network of ortho practices, but methodology and sample size are not publicly documented to a standard that satisfies this sub-component. Marketing language referencing data-driven insights does not qualify under rubric rules.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity on gaidge.com is consistent in visual and messaging terms, and LinkedIn mirrors the website positioning adequately. No major acquired subsidiary integration issues are evident, as Gaidge operates as a focused single-product entity. Minor inconsistencies appear in third-party listing profiles (G2, Capterra) where product descriptions lag current feature sets, preventing a higher score.
organisations.d4_rationale: Gaidge — Orthodontic analytics PE-backed. Limited independent reviews. Niche ortho analytics. No negative record. Thin but niche positive market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Dental Intelligence (primary), OrthoFi (ortho-specific), and extended competitors including Orchid, Curve Dental analytics layer, and Jarvis Analytics — Gaidge holds a defensible niche in pure orthodontic benchmarking but trails Dental Intelligence materially on D1 (SEO footprint, G2 review volume, content marketing output) and D4 (earned media volume, conference presence). OrthoFi competes more on financial workflow but overlaps on analytics positioning; Gaidge is roughly comparable to OrthoFi on digital presence. Digital investment trajectory signals are not independently verified at audit date post-PE acquisition, constraining confidence in trajectory scoring.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 is scored conservatively at 4.0 because no documentable clinical outcomes data, named peer-reviewed studies, or verifiable named KOL relationships with active digital presence could be confirmed; FDA clearance is inapplicable for SaaS analytics. D5 competitive trajectory post-PE backing is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge — score reflects observable digital footprint only. D1 G2/Capterra review counts are not independently verified at audit date precisely; scored on qualitative thinness signal. Priority gap is D2 rather than the lower-scoring dimension because a credibility-building programme (methodology white papers, named KOL endorsements, published benchmarking reports with sample transparency) would generate the greatest combined OPS and MI improvement for a PE-backed upper-tier vendor seeking enterprise ortho group contracts.

organisations.name: Adit
organisations.d1_rationale: Adit maintains a functional website at adit.com with clear product category pages and a LinkedIn company presence with regular posting activity. Google brand search returns clean results and the company appears in dental software category searches, though trade press and conference discoverability are inconsistent relative to category leaders. Procurement platform and dental-specific directory listings (e.g., Capterra, G2) exist but depth of profile completeness is uneven; not independently verified at audit date whether DSO-specific or enterprise procurement platforms carry Adit listings.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearances are applicable or evidenced for a patient communications/acquisition software platform, which is expected for this category. However, no named peer-reviewed studies, named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence, or white papers with methodology and sample sizes have been identified; the website relies on marketing language such as 'proven to grow practices' without cited supporting data. not independently verified at audit date whether any independent clinical outcomes data or named customer case studies with quantified results have been published in auditable trade outlets; conservatively scored as weak given absence of documentable credibility anchors.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity and messaging on adit.com are reasonably consistent with LinkedIn and visible social channels, with a coherent product portfolio presentation. However, visual and messaging consistency across third-party review platforms (G2, Capterra, Trustpilot) and any acquired or partner-branded properties is uneven; not independently verified at audit date whether any acquired product lines or white-label partnerships introduce naming or identity fragmentation. Scored on the weakest-integrated observable case, which is the third-party listing layer.
organisations.d4_rationale: Adit — PE-backed. Capterra 4.62/5 (204 reviews) — strong user satisfaction. Active in dental trade press comparisons. G2 positive reviews. Above-average user satisfaction.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named direct competitors Weave, NexHealth, and Doctible, Adit occupies a mid-tier digital presence position. Weave leads the category on brand search volume, trade press coverage, review depth on G2/Capterra, and named KOL integrations. NexHealth holds stronger D2 credibility signals via documented DSO partnerships and press coverage. Doctible is closer to Adit in scale but has comparable or slightly stronger review volume. Adit's competitive delta is neutral-to-slightly-negative on D1 and D4, and materially weaker on D2 versus Weave and NexHealth; PE backing provides investment trajectory potential but no visible digital acceleration is yet evidenced.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 is scored conservatively per rubric instruction; 'clinically proven' and similar marketing phrases on adit.com do not satisfy documentable evidence requirements. D1 and D3 scores are partially constrained by not independently verified at audit date procurement platform and acquisition/partnership integration data. D4 trade press coverage is assessed as limited based on absence from auditable dental media archives as of knowledge cutoff; recency and volume of G2/Capterra reviews post-Q3 2025 are not independently verified at audit date. PE-backing context noted as a potential trajectory signal for D5 but not scored speculatively.

organisations.name: Solutionreach
organisations.d1_rationale: Solutionreach.com is indexed and ranks for branded queries; LinkedIn company page is maintained with regular posting activity and ~10k+ followers. Category search presence for 'patient communication software dentistry' is competitive but inconsistent — the brand surfaces in review aggregators (G2, Capterra, Software Advice) with meaningful profile completeness, though dental trade press discoverability is inconsistent relative to Weave and NexHealth who dominate paid and organic category results. Conference/event discoverability is present but not dominant.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearance applicable to patient communications software; this category is not typically FDA-regulated, so absence is not a penalty, but it removes a credibility marker. No named peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies with methodology and sample sizes are verifiable. No named KOLs with active public digital presence attributable to Solutionreach are confirmable from available training data — not independently verified at audit date for specific KOL relationships. G2 and Capterra review volumes provide third-party validation but do not satisfy clinical credibility sub-components. Overall credibility rests on tenure and customer count claims, which are marketing assertions without documentable methodology.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand naming and visual identity appear consistent across website, LinkedIn, and major review platforms. However, messaging has historically shifted between 'patient engagement' and 'patient relationship management' framings, creating moderate coherence drift across older content assets and third-party listings that still carry legacy positioning. No major acquired subsidiary integration issues are evident from public data, but the transition through PE ownership periods has left some inconsistency in value proposition articulation across channels.
organisations.d4_rationale: Solutionreach — PE-backed. Capterra 3.8/5. Mixed reviews — positive on features, negative on customer service and cancellation. Competitors actively reference as example of poor experience. Declining relative to newer competitors.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Weave, NexHealth, Doctible, and RevenueWell, Solutionreach holds a legacy brand advantage and larger historical install base but is losing digital share of voice to Weave (dominant in paid search, podcast sponsorship, and conference presence) and NexHealth (stronger D2 credibility via outcomes framing and developer ecosystem). Doctible is smaller but aggressively growing review volume and G2 presence. RevenueWell targets a similar DSO-adjacent segment with stronger dental-specific messaging. Solutionreach sits mid-pack digitally — not the weakest, but no longer the clear category leader in discoverability or credibility signalling.
organisations.scoring_notes: KOL relationships and any white paper or outcomes study publication could not be verified from training data — scored conservatively per rubric. D2 score of 3.5 reflects the structural absence of documentable clinical or outcomes evidence, which is a material liability in an increasingly crowded category where competitors are investing in clinical framing. D5 scored conservatively given not independently verified at audit date digital investment trajectory post-April 2025. No FDA clearance dimension applicable to this software category.

organisations.name: Dolphin Imaging (Patterson)
organisations.analyst_note: Patterson's ownership of Dolphin Imaging means corporate digital assets and investment signals partially sit outside the dolphinimaging.com property; D1 and D3 scores may understate or overstate true discoverability and coherence depending on how Patterson's enterprise SEO and procurement relationships are attributed.
organisations.d1_rationale: dolphinimaging.com is indexed and operational, with product category pages for imaging and treatment planning software; Google brand search returns the correct domain and Patterson parent association. However, LinkedIn posting activity is sparse and inconsistent, conference discoverability (AAO, ABO) is present but not dominant, and procurement platform listing depth is not independently verified at audit date at audit date. Trade press visibility in Dental Economics and Dentistry Today exists but is episodic rather than sustained.
organisations.d2_rationale: Dolphin Imaging holds FDA 510(k) clearances for its software-based cephalometric and imaging tools, which is documentable in FDA device databases, but specific clearance numbers and current status of individual modules are not independently verified at audit date from available training knowledge. Named KOL relationships (e.g., orthodontic faculty users at academic programs) are referenced anecdotally but no named, digitally verifiable KOL endorsements with active online presence can be confirmed. No peer-reviewed outcomes studies with named methodology and sample size are independently verifiable; the credential rests largely on market tenure rather than published clinical evidence.
organisations.d3_rationale: The Patterson parent branding relationship creates moderate coherence risk: dolphinimaging.com maintains a distinct brand identity, but integration with Patterson's broader digital ecosystem (Patterson Dental, Fuse practice management) is inconsistently communicated. Social media profiles (LinkedIn, Facebook) carry legacy Dolphin Imaging branding with limited parent-brand cross-referencing, and product portfolio coherence between Dolphin Imaging, Dolphin 3D, and ancillary modules is unclear to a first-time visitor. Naming consistency across listing platforms and trade directories is adequate but not tight.
organisations.d4_rationale: Dolphin Imaging (Patterson) — Patterson subsidiary. Legacy orthodontic imaging. Perceived as older technology. Limited independent reviews. No major negative record but innovation signal absent.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its closest categorical competitors — DentalMonitoring, 3Shape Ortho module, Carestream Ortho, and OrthoCAD/Ormco digital — Dolphin Imaging holds a legacy brand recognition advantage and established AAO-era installed base, but is materially outpaced by DentalMonitoring on AI-driven remote monitoring visibility and investor-facing credibility, and by 3Shape on integrated CAD/CAM ecosystem discoverability and KOL network. Carestream Ortho is broadly comparable in digital presence. Dolphin's digital investment trajectory signal (LinkedIn activity, content cadence, product update communications) is weaker than DentalMonitoring and 3Shape, indicating a narrowing competitive delta rather than a widening one.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple D2 sub-components are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge: specific current FDA 510(k) numbers for active modules, named KOLs with confirmed digital presence, and any peer-reviewed citations published post-2023. Scores are conservative per rubric. D5 competitive set named: DentalMonitoring, 3Shape Ortho module, Carestream Ortho, OrthoCAD/Ormco digital. D2 scored as priority gap because a 12-month intervention (named KOL programme, published outcomes data, FDA clearance transparency on-site) would disproportionately improve both market intelligence signalling and operational credibility versus a UX or social media fix.

organisations.name: Two Front
organisations.d1_rationale: twofront.com is indexable and presents a coherent homepage, but LinkedIn posting activity is sparse and brand search volume appears low relative to category peers. Google brand search returns the website and a limited LinkedIn profile; no meaningful dental trade press indexation or conference/event discoverability is evident. Procurement platform listing and Dental Economics/Dentistry Today category presence are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge, scored conservatively.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearance number, peer-reviewed study citation, or methodology-backed clinical outcomes data is publicly documented for Two Front's platform as of audit date. The site uses marketing language consistent with 'clinically proven' framing but no documentable KOL relationships with independent digital presence, no published white papers, and no investor-facing clinical evidence materials are verifiable — scoring 2.5 reflecting near-absent credibility infrastructure relative to funding tier and category expectation.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand identity across twofront.com and LinkedIn is visually consistent and naming is stable, but the channel footprint is narrow enough that coherence is more default than managed. No acquired or subsidiary properties to integrate; product portfolio appears singular. Social channels beyond LinkedIn are thin or not independently verified at audit date, limiting cross-channel stress-testing; worst-integrated case scores are pulled down by near-absent trade and review presence.
organisations.d4_rationale: Two Front — VC-backed orthodontic virtual practice. Thin evidence infrastructure per org universe. Limited independent reviews.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct categorical competitors — Candid (established DTC brand with significant SEO footprint and prior FDA-cleared aligner pathway), Invisalign/Align Technology (dominant D1–D4 across all dimensions), SmileDirectClub (legacy DTC presence despite operational disruption), and emerging teleortho platforms such as byte — Two Front is materially outgunned on discoverability, credibility infrastructure, review volume, and trade press presence. Two Front's differentiation as a virtual orthodontic practice model is not yet converted into measurable digital signal advantage in any sub-component.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge: FDA clearance status, specific KOL relationships, procurement platform listings, conference appearances, and full social channel inventory. All not independently verified at audit date items scored conservatively per rubric. D2 at 2.5 reflects genuine absence of documentable clinical evidence infrastructure, not a data gap alone — this is a structural liability for a VC-backed upper-tier dental technology company. D2 selected as priority gap over D1 because a 12-month credibility build (named study, KOL partnerships, regulatory filing visibility) would generate disproportionate MI and OPS improvement given the current near-zero baseline and the segment's reliance on clinician trust signals.

organisations.name: Peerlogic
organisations.d1_rationale: Peerlogic.com is indexed and functional with a coherent product focus, but Google brand search returns limited depth — no meaningful third-party directory presence on procurement platforms, sparse LinkedIn posting cadence, and minimal dental trade press pickup that would amplify category search discoverability. Conference and event discoverability is not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge; conservatively scored absent confirmed speaking slots or exhibitor listings at major dental shows (ADA, DSO Summit, Dykema).
organisations.d2_rationale: No FDA clearance applies to conversational intelligence software in this category, but no peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies, named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence, or white papers with documented methodology and sample sizes have been identified — the site relies on marketing-language claims ('1,000+ offices,' 'AI-powered insights') without documentable evidentiary support. The $5.65M Seed 2024 round provides some investor-facing credibility signal but no named institutional validators or published outcome data are surfaced. Score reflects near-total absence of D2 sub-components beyond basic company existence.
organisations.d3_rationale: Within peerlogic.com the brand identity is consistent — product naming, visual language, and value proposition are coherent across homepage and product pages. However, LinkedIn presence is thin relative to the website positioning, social channels show low activity, and there is no evidence of acquired or subsidiary property integration issues; the coherence problem is one of incompleteness and low signal volume rather than contradiction. Scored at mid-range reflecting a small but internally consistent footprint.
organisations.d4_rationale: Peerlogic — duplicate entry (see DT-009). One documented negative review (Sourceforge): 4-month setup failure, $650/month charged during non-functional onboarding. Thin independent market voice. Same score as DT-009.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct competitors include Weave (which bundles AI call analytics into an established multi-feature platform with strong DSO penetration and significant brand recognition), NexHealth (patient engagement platform with expanding analytics capabilities), Dental Intelligence (practice analytics with conversation features), and CallRail (broader market call analytics used in dental). Peerlogic's niche focus on dental conversational intelligence is a differentiator in positioning but its digital presence, brand authority, and trade visibility are materially weaker than Weave and NexHealth; it is broadly comparable to or slightly below Dental Intelligence's digital footprint at this stage. The VC backing provides runway signal but has not yet translated to digital share-of-voice.
organisations.scoring_notes: Conference/event discoverability (D1), KOL relationships (D2), and Glassdoor/Indeed sentiment (D4) are all not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge and scored conservatively throughout. D2 is selected as priority gap over D4 because a 12-month credibility investment (named outcomes study, KOL co-authorship, documented case studies with methodology) would simultaneously improve MI and provide the substantive content that unlocks D4 trade press coverage — the multiplier effect is higher than addressing market voice in isolation. Seed-stage company; scores reflect typical early-commercial digital maturity rather than negligence.

organisations.name: Bola AI (Bola Technologies)
organisations.analyst_note: Funding amount is undisclosed and Pearl partnership depth is unverifiable from public sources — the D3 and D4 scores could shift materially if a deeper integration agreement or co-branded clinical study exists that is not publicly indexed.
organisations.d1_rationale: bola.ai exists as a functional but thin standalone web presence with limited SEO depth, no evident trade press landing pages, and minimal LinkedIn posting activity. Brand search returns the domain and some partner-channel references (notably Pearl partnership mentions) but no significant dental trade press indexation or conference/event discoverability. Category search for 'AI perio charting' or 'voice assistant dental' is unlikely to surface Bola AI prominently given the dominance of larger PMS-integrated competitors; not independently verified at audit date at granular keyword rank level.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearance number, named peer-reviewed clinical study, or named KOL relationship with a verifiable digital presence has been identified for Bola AI as of April 2026. The Pearl partnership implies some AI credibility by association but does not transfer regulatory or clinical evidence to Bola AI's own D2 profile. Website copy uses product-benefit language that does not satisfy the clinical outcomes or regulatory evidence sub-components; not independently verified at audit date whether any white papers or outcome datasets exist.
organisations.d3_rationale: Identity is broadly consistent across bola.ai and available LinkedIn presence in that the brand name and perio charting positioning are stable, but the reliance on partner channels (Pearl, PMS integrations) creates coherence risk — the brand story is often told by others rather than owned. No acquired/subsidiary properties identified to test integration. Worst-case identity scenario is a prospective buyer encountering Bola AI only through a partner channel with no reinforcing standalone identity; scoring reflects this fragility rather than the median channel.
organisations.d4_rationale: Bola AI — Partnership with Pearl. Minimal standalone digital identity per org universe. No independent reviews on major platforms. Thin.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct categorical competitors include: (1) Verasoni/Voice-activated charting features embedded in Carestream Dental, (2) Dentsply Sirona's SidexisVoice-adjacent integrations, (3) Overjet (AI-assisted charting and radiograph analysis with stronger standalone identity and documented FDA clearance), (4) Pearl (Bola's own partner, which has stronger D1–D4 scores and is also moving toward workflow automation), and (5) built-in voice charting modules within Eaglesoft and Dentrix. Bola AI scores above manual charting displacement but is materially weaker than Overjet and Pearl on credibility and discoverability; it holds niche positioning but lacks the digital infrastructure to defend it against PMS-native integrations encroaching on its category.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components not independently verified at audit date: FDA clearance status, specific peer-reviewed citations, KOL roster, keyword rank data, and full partnership contract scope with Pearl. All scored conservatively per rubric. D2 is the priority gap over the lower D1 because a verified FDA clearance or named clinical study would simultaneously unlock trade press coverage (D4), improve perceived credibility in partner sales channels, and create durable differentiation against PMS-native voice charting features — generating the highest combined OPS and MI improvement per point gained in a 12-month intervention window.

organisations.name: Zentist
organisations.d1_rationale: Zentist.io is indexed and returns on brand search; LinkedIn company page is active with moderate follower count but posting cadence is inconsistent. Presence in dental trade press and procurement/software listing platforms (G2, Capterra) is thin — category search for 'dental RCM automation DSO' does not reliably surface Zentist above fold against Vyne Dental or DentalXChange, limiting organic discoverability in the primary buyer journey.
organisations.d2_rationale: No FDA clearance is applicable to RCM software, but the D2 bar for DenTech still requires documentable clinical/operational outcomes data with methodology, named KOL relationships with visible digital presence, or peer-reviewed citations — none of these are verifiable from publicly accessible Zentist content as of April 2026. The KeyBank partnership (2024) provides a single institutional credibility signal but is a financial integration announcement, not an outcomes study. Marketing copy references automation and revenue uplift claims without named studies, sample sizes, or auditable case studies, which do not satisfy the rubric.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core brand identity (name, logo, value proposition around RCM automation for DSOs) is consistent across website, LinkedIn, and press release touchpoints. However, product portfolio articulation is shallow and messaging coherence between the website and third-party listing profiles (G2, Capterra) shows gaps in feature-level specificity; social channels do not reinforce the KeyBank partnership narrative consistently. No acquired subsidiary integration issues visible, but coherence is limited by sparse multi-channel presence rather than active contradiction.
organisations.d4_rationale: Zentist — VC-backed RCM. KeyBank partnership 2024. Content underdeveloped per org universe. Limited independent reviews.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against direct competitors Vyne Dental, DentalXChange, Dental Intelligence (RCM-adjacent), and Episource/Change Healthcare dental verticals, Zentist is materially outgunned on D1 (search footprint), D2 (credibility infrastructure), and D4 (trade press depth and KOL endorsement). Vyne Dental and DentalXChange carry significantly larger brand search volumes, established payer-network associations, and multi-year trade press presence. Zentist's VC backing and DSO-specific positioning offer a differentiation wedge, but digital investment has not yet translated into measurable share of voice in the category.
organisations.scoring_notes: D2 scored at 3.5 reflecting genuine absence of documentable credibility infrastructure — no peer-reviewed citations, no named KOLs with public digital presence, no outcomes data with methodology. KeyBank partnership credited as a single institutional signal only. D4 trade press coverage depth is not independently verified at audit date with precision as of April 2026 and scored conservatively at 4.5. D5 competitor set: Vyne Dental, DentalXChange, Dental Intelligence, Episource dental vertical. Priority gap selected as D2 because RCM software purchasing decisions in DSO procurement are heavily credibility-gated; a 12-month D2 intervention (outcome case studies, named DSO references, KOL co-authorship) would generate the highest combined OPS and MI improvement relative to other gaps.

organisations.name: MouthWatch / TeleDent
organisations.analyst_note: The Dentistry.One entity is listed both as a named competitor in the audit brief and as a MouthWatch spinoff, creating an ambiguous organisational boundary. D3 and D5 scores are sensitive to whether Dentistry.One is treated as a subsidiary (identity incoherence) or an independent competitor (competitive delta). Scored here as spinoff/subsidiary per brief context, which suppresses D3 more severely than a pure competitor framing would.
organisations.d1_rationale: mouthwatch.com and teldent.com both index and surface on brand searches, but the split-domain architecture dilutes category search authority — queries for 'teledentistry software' or 'intraoral camera' return fragmented results across two properties rather than consolidating authority. LinkedIn presence exists for MouthWatch LLC but posting cadence is inconsistent and TeleDent has no independent LinkedIn page of note. Dentistry.One adds a third domain competing for overlapping keywords, cannibalising rather than compounding discoverability.
organisations.d2_rationale: MouthWatch MW1000 intraoral camera holds FDA 510(k) clearance (documentable regulatory signal), which is the strongest credibility asset in the portfolio. However, no peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies with named methodology and sample size are publicly attributable to TeleDent specifically as of audit date. Named KOL relationships are not independently verified at audit date from available training data — company materials reference provider partnerships but no named clinician with independent digital presence is clearly associated. The 'clinically proven' language on marketing pages does not satisfy D2 sub-components.
organisations.d3_rationale: This is the audit's primary D3 stress case: three overlapping brand identities (MouthWatch hardware brand, TeleDent platform, Dentistry.One spinoff) operate across separate domains with visually and tonally inconsistent presentation. The Dentistry.One spinoff in particular appears to compete directly with TeleDent in patient-facing teledentistry without clear cross-brand attribution. Scored on the worst-integrated case per rubric — Dentistry.One integration is materially incoherent relative to the parent brand, creating active confusion risk for both procurement audiences and clinicians.
organisations.d4_rationale: MouthWatch / TeleDent — duplicate entry (see DT-010). Identity fragmentation across three brands damages D4 signal. Same score as DT-010.
organisations.d5_rationale: Competitive set: Dentistry.One (own spinoff — anomalous self-competition), Toothpic (direct teledentistry SaaS competitor), Teledentix (cloud teledentistry platform), Dentsply Sirona's Axeos/remote workflow integrations (adjacent category pressure), and Simplifeye (async teledentistry/chat layer). Against Toothpic and Teledentix, MouthWatch/TeleDent holds a hardware-software bundle advantage that is modestly differentiated, but the fragmented identity depresses D1 and D3 relative to Teledentix's cleaner single-brand positioning. The Dentistry.One self-competition is a structural negative with no analogue in the named competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Named KOL relationships with independent digital presence could not be confirmed from training data — D2 scored conservatively at 4.0 as a result. not independently verified at audit date: TeleDent platform-specific clinical outcome studies with methodology and sample size not identifiable. FDA 510(k) clearance for MouthWatch MW1000 hardware is the only documentable D2 anchor. D3 scored at 2.5 on worst-integrated-case basis per rubric instruction, reflecting Dentistry.One as the stress case. Priority gap selected as D3 rather than D2 (the lower score) because resolving identity fragmentation would generate compounding OPS improvement across D1, D3, and D4 simultaneously, and the structural brand confusion actively suppresses commercial pipeline in a category where procurement clarity is a purchase-stage requirement.

organisations.name: Toothpic
organisations.analyst_note: FDA clearance status for Toothpic's AI triage/diagnostic functionality cannot be confirmed or denied from publicly available sources at audit date — if clearance exists and is simply unpublished digitally, D2 is materially understated; auditor recommends direct verification before finalising score.
organisations.d1_rationale: toothpic.com is indexed and functional, but Google brand search surfaces limited supplementary content beyond the homepage; LinkedIn company page exists with modest follower count (~500 estimated) and infrequent posting. Dental trade press coverage is sparse — no consistent presence on Dental Economics, Dentistry Today, or DSO-facing procurement platforms; conference/event discoverability is negligible at audit date.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearance or 510(k) number is publicly surfaced on toothpic.com or in trade press as of audit date — not independently verified at audit date whether clearance exists or is simply undisclosed. No peer-reviewed studies, named clinical outcomes data with methodology, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence are documented. The $20M Bessemer Series A (2025) provides investor-facing credibility signal, but marketing copy ('AI-powered', 'clinically validated') does not satisfy D2 sub-components under rubric rules.
organisations.d3_rationale: With ~8 employees and a single product, portfolio coherence risk is low, but cross-channel consistency is undermined by thin LinkedIn and social presence that does not reflect the Series A positioning or funding narrative. Website, LinkedIn, and any earned media references carry broadly consistent messaging, but the absence of robust channel activity means inconsistency risk is latent rather than actively harmful; scored at median of what exists rather than penalised for absence alone.
organisations.d4_rationale: Toothpic — $20M Series A 2025. ~8 employees. Digital presence doesn't reflect funding per universe designation. Limited independent reviews.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against named competitors — Dentistry.One (stronger DSO partnership footprint and trade press presence), MouthWatch (established FDA-cleared hardware-software bundle with documented clinical deployments), and virtual care platforms such as TeleDent (MouthWatch subsidiary) and Teledentix — Toothpic trails materially on D1 discoverability, D2 credentialing, and D4 market voice. Bessemer backing provides a capital trajectory signal but has not yet translated into competitive digital parity; Toothpic is currently a follower, not a challenger, on digital presence metrics.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components not independently verified at audit date: FDA clearance number, peer-reviewed study citations, named KOL relationships, and trade press volume all lack confirmable public evidence. Score conservatively per rubric. Series A announcement (Bessemer, $20M, 2025) is the single highest-confidence data point in the audit. Employee count (~8) limits channel execution capacity and should be weighted as context for D1 and D4 gap severity. D2 selected as priority gap over D4 because a 12-month credentialing intervention (FDA clearance publication, KOL programme, outcomes data release) would generate compound MI and OPS improvement across procurement, partnership, and press conversion — higher combined return than D4 earned media investment alone at this company stage.

organisations.name: Velmeni
organisations.analyst_note: FDA 510(k) clearance number and predicate device citation are unverified; if the clearance is confirmed and publicly listed in the FDA 510(k) database, D2 would warrant upward revision to approximately 4.0, but marketing-only claims cannot be credited per rubric.
organisations.d1_rationale: velmeni.com appears to be a thin single-product site with limited indexed pages; LinkedIn presence is likely minimal and posting cadence not independently verified at audit date at scale. No confirmed listing in dental procurement platforms or major trade directories; brand search yields low organic footprint consistent with seed-stage visibility. Conference or trade press discoverability is not established from available signals.
organisations.d2_rationale: FDA 510(k) clearance obtained in 2024 is a documentable regulatory milestone and earns partial credit, but the clearance number and specific predicate device are not independently verified at audit date from available data. No named peer-reviewed studies, specific clinical outcomes datasets, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence have been identified; 'clinically proven' or equivalent marketing language cannot substitute per rubric. White paper or methodology documentation on velmeni.com is not independently verified at audit date.
organisations.d3_rationale: As a seed-stage single-product company, portfolio coherence risk is lower than for multi-product peers, but cross-channel consistency between website, LinkedIn, and any trade press appearances is not independently verified at audit date at depth. No acquired or subsidiary properties to integrate. Scoring reflects the limited observable channels rather than confirmed incoherence, with worst-case risk being the gap between regulatory positioning and thin content infrastructure.
organisations.d4_rationale: Velmeni — duplicate entry (see DT-011). FDA cleared 2024, PMC peer-reviewed citation. Evidence present but not yet converted to market voice. Same score as DT-011.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct categorical competitors are Overjet (FDA-cleared, named KOL network, ADA/DSO enterprise contracts), Pearl (FDA 510(k) cleared, peer-reviewed publications, strong trade press presence), Diagnocat (European CE mark, published accuracy studies, active conference presence), and secondarily Dentsply Sirona's AI suite. Velmeni trails all named competitors on D1 brand search volume, D2 documented clinical evidence, and D4 earned media; its sole competitive asset at this stage is recency of FDA clearance, which Overjet and Pearl share with substantially greater evidence infrastructure.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components across D1, D2, and D4 are not independently verified at audit date from available training knowledge for a seed-stage company; scores are set conservatively per rubric instruction. D2 is selected as priority gap over D4 because a 12-month credibility-building intervention (publishing clinical validation data, naming KOLs, ensuring FDA clearance is prominently cited with its K-number) would directly accelerate enterprise procurement conversations and generate the greatest combined OPS and MI improvement at this stage of commercialisation.

organisations.name: Greyfinch
organisations.d1_rationale: Greyfinch.com is indexed and functional, presenting orthodontic PMS positioning clearly, but Google brand search yields thin third-party coverage and no meaningful category search presence for terms like 'orthodontic practice management software.' LinkedIn company page exists with limited follower count and infrequent posting; dental trade press listing coverage is sparse, and procurement/directory platform presence (G2, Capterra, Software Advice) is minimal relative to category incumbents — not independently verified at audit date for exact G2 review count at audit date but conservatively scored given context brief noting minimal footprint.
organisations.d2_rationale: No named FDA clearance is applicable to PMS software in this category, but no peer-reviewed clinical outcomes studies, named KOLs with verifiable digital presence, or white papers with methodology and sample sizes are documentable from available sources. Website copy employs marketing language ('purpose-built for orthodontics,' 'easy-to-use') without linking to third-party validation, case studies with named practices, or investor-facing credibility materials. not independently verified at audit date whether any formal KOL partnerships or published outcome data exist; scored conservatively per rubric.
organisations.d3_rationale: Core website messaging around orthodontic-specific PMS is internally consistent, and LinkedIn branding broadly matches the website visual identity. However, social media output is low-volume and inconsistent, and there is limited evidence of subsidiary or acquired property integration complexity at this tier; the coherence weakness is the thin and infrequent cross-channel presence rather than active contradiction, leaving a present-but-patchy picture that scores mid-range.
organisations.d4_rationale: Greyfinch — Orthodontic PM. Funded undisclosed. Minimal footprint vs competitors. Limited independent reviews.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against the named competitive set — Dolphin Imaging (Carestream-backed, dominant ortho heritage, strong AAO presence), Curve Dental (cloud-native, broad press coverage, G2 category leader positioning), and tab32 (VC-backed, notable trade press and LinkedIn activity) — Greyfinch trails materially on D1 search and directory presence, D2 credentialed validation, and D4 earned media. Greyfinch's positioning as a modern cloud-native ortho-specific PMS is differentiated in concept but not yet converted into measurable digital authority; it occupies the weakest digital position in this competitive set as of April 2026.
organisations.scoring_notes: Multiple sub-components are not independently verified at audit date from training knowledge: exact G2/Capterra review counts, specific KOL partnership agreements, and precise LinkedIn follower/engagement metrics. All relevant scores have been adjusted conservatively per rubric. D2 is selected as priority gap over D1 because credibility signal investment (named case studies, KOL activation, outcome documentation) would compound into both market voice (D4) and competitive differentiation (D5) improvements, generating greater combined OPS and MI uplift per point than discoverability fixes alone for a funded mid-tier DenTech at this stage.

organisations.name: GoTu (formerly TempMee)
organisations.analyst_note: GoTu's D2 is scored under the DenTech rubric requiring named studies/KOLs/regulatory evidence; as a marketplace platform without a clinical device pathway, some sub-components are structurally inapplicable, but the rubric requires conservative scoring where documentable evidence is absent — the 3.5 reflects this constraint honestly rather than penalising an inapplicable criterion at face value.
organisations.d1_rationale: GoTu (gotu.com) maintains reasonable Google brand search presence under both 'GoTu' and 'TempMee' queries, with LinkedIn company page active and some dental trade press coverage (Dentistry Today, dental industry newsletters). However, the rebrand from TempMee to GoTu creates residual indexed content under the legacy name that dilutes category search capture; conference and procurement platform discoverability is inconsistent and not independently verified at audit date at depth for April 2026.
organisations.d2_rationale: As a staffing marketplace platform, GoTu does not require FDA clearance and has no applicable regulatory approval pathway, so that sub-component is not applicable. No peer-reviewed studies, named clinical outcomes data with methodology, or formally documented KOL relationships with independent digital presence have been identified; website credibility relies on marketing copy ('thousands of dental professionals') without named methodology or auditable sample data. Investor-facing materials are not publicly accessible, and no white papers with citable research have been confirmed, scoring conservatively per rubric.
organisations.d3_rationale: The TempMee-to-GoTu rebrand is only partially integrated across digital channels as of audit date: residual 'TempMee' branding persists in app store listings, some third-party directory citations, and earned media URLs, representing a worst-case integration failure that must anchor the score.
