organisations.name: Planet DDS Apteryx Imaging
organisations.analyst_note: Dual-brand structure (Apteryx + Planet DDS) makes isolated scoring of D1 and D3 boundary cases — some signals attributable to Planet DDS parent may inflate or deflate Apteryx-specific scores; scoring reflects conservative Apteryx-attributable evidence only.
organisations.d1_rationale: Apteryx.com maintains a functional web presence and appears indexed for core category terms such as 'dental imaging software' and 'cloud dental imaging,' and Planet DDS provides some umbrella brand lift; however, apteryx.com operates as a semi-independent subdomain/brand within the Planet DDS ecosystem, creating split search equity and inconsistent category landing-page depth. LinkedIn presence exists under both Apteryx and Planet DDS handles, diluting consolidated brand search. Trade press discoverability in Dental Economics and Dentistry Today is present but not dominant, and conference/event discoverability is uneven relative to category leaders.
organisations.d2_rationale: No documentable FDA clearance numbers, peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies, or named KOL relationships with verifiable digital presence could be confirmed for Apteryx Imaging as a distinct product line at audit date — not independently verified at audit date for specific clearance numbers. Planet DDS marketing materials use phrases such as 'cloud-native imaging' and reference HIPAA compliance but these do not satisfy D2 sub-components. No white papers with methodology and sample sizes were identified on apteryx.com or planetdds.com. Investor-facing credibility materials exist at the Planet DDS level but do not isolate Apteryx Imaging clinical claims with documentable evidence.
organisations.d3_rationale: The dual-brand architecture — Apteryx as legacy product identity and Planet DDS as parent — creates measurable coherence friction: apteryx.com and planetdds.com carry overlapping but inconsistently aligned messaging, with some Apteryx pages not prominently featuring Planet DDS branding and vice versa. Social handles, LinkedIn pages, and trade press bylines reference both brands without a clear hierarchy, and the worst-integrated case (standalone apteryx.com pages) lacks updated Planet DDS visual identity as of available audit signals. Contact and support routing shows some disjointed UX between the two properties.
organisations.d4_rationale: Planet DDS Apteryx Imaging — Dental imaging component of Planet DDS. Apteryx brand has established clinician presence. Integration with Planet DDS PMS. No major negative record. Mid-range market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against closest categorical competitors — Carestream Dental Imaging (CS Imaging, strong brand recognition, dedicated FDA-cleared product pages, active KOL network), Planmeca Imaging (strong European and North American trade presence, named clinical publications, hardware-software integration narrative), Dentsply Sirona (broad imaging portfolio with regulatory documentation and conference dominance), and Eaglesoft/Patterson (distribution-backed discoverability) — Apteryx Imaging trails materially on D2 (documentable clinical evidence) and D4 (named KOL and trade press volume). D1 is competitive at the cloud-native DSO segment level via Planet DDS but lags in standalone imaging category search. Digital investment trajectory signal through Planet DDS is positive but Apteryx-specific momentum is not clearly differentiated.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA clearance numbers for Apteryx imaging products not independently verified at audit date from available training knowledge — scored conservatively at D2. Named KOL relationships not independently verified at audit date. G2/Capterra imaging-specific review volume not independently verified at audit date in isolation from Planet DDS aggregate. D5 competitive set confirmed as: Carestream Dental Imaging, Planmeca Imaging, Dentsply Sirona Imaging, Patterson/Eaglesoft imaging integration. Tier classification as Upper acknowledged but scores reflect documented digital presence gaps relative to category leaders, not product capability.

organisations.name: uLab Systems
organisations.d1_rationale: ulabsystems.com is indexable and presents a coherent product-focused site, with LinkedIn presence and some dental trade press mentions confirming basic discoverability. However, conference and procurement platform visibility is limited relative to larger competitors; category search for 'clear aligner planning software' surfaces Align SureSmile and 3Shape more prominently than uLab. not independently verified at audit date: precise Google ranking positions and Dental Trade Alliance or procurement platform listing status cannot be confirmed from training data.
organisations.d2_rationale: uLab holds FDA 510(k) clearance for its uDesign software platform (clearance status documentable from FDA device database), which provides a foundational credibility signal. However, named peer-reviewed clinical outcome studies with methodology and sample sizes are not prominently surfaced on the site or in indexed dental literature as of training cutoff; KOL relationships exist (clinician case studies appear on their site) but named KOLs with independently verifiable digital presence are sparse. White papers present but lack rigorous citation scaffolding, so the D2 floor is constrained despite regulatory foundation.
organisations.d3_rationale: Brand and visual identity are consistent across website, LinkedIn, and trade press appearances, with no major subsidiary or acquisition integration burden evident. Messaging around 'doctor-driven aligner design' is applied consistently across channels. Minor inconsistencies exist in product naming (uDesign, uAssist, uLab) which creates occasional coherence friction for a first-time evaluator, but no severely misaligned acquired properties detected.
organisations.d4_rationale: uLab Systems — Orthodontic treatment planning software. Active in orthodontic trade press. Conference presence documented. Growing clinician adoption narrative. No major negative record. Above-average market voice for ortho niche.
organisations.d5_rationale: Against its named competitive set — Candid Pro (DSO-facing channel), Align SureSmile (enterprise orthodontic workflow), 3Shape aligner module (CAD-integrated), and secondarily Ormco Spark digital tools — uLab occupies a differentiated but digitally under-amplified position. Align SureSmile and 3Shape materially outperform on D1 (search visibility, procurement listing) and D2 (published outcomes, regulatory portfolio breadth). Candid Pro's DSO focus limits direct overlap but its marketing investment outpaces uLab. uLab's doctor-control positioning is a genuine differentiator but is not yet converted into measurable digital share-of-voice advantage.
organisations.scoring_notes: FDA 510(k) clearance for uDesign is the strongest verifiable D2 anchor; specific clearance number (K-number) not confirmed from training data — scored conservatively. KOL digital presence and peer-reviewed citation count are not independently verified at audit date at study-level specificity. D5 competitive delta scoring is constrained by limited public digital investment trajectory data for uLab (VC-backed, private). Priority gap assigned to D2 because a 12-month intervention — publishing named clinical outcome data, surfacing clearance documentation prominently, formalising KOL programme — would simultaneously improve MI and D4/D5 downstream, offering the highest combined OPS and MI uplift per dimension.

organisations.name: Overjet (Insurers Division)
organisations.analyst_note: This entry is flagged as a sub-division of DT-011 (Overjet, clinician/DSO-facing). Scoring here reflects the insurer-division product line specifically; where evidence is indistinguishable between divisions, the unified entity record is used and conservatively applied. If DT-011 has already been scored, D1–D5 deltas between the two records should be reviewed for consistency before publishing.
organisations.d1_rationale: Overjet maintains a functional website with a dedicated insurers/payers section and active LinkedIn presence (~10k+ followers as of late 2025), and appears in dental trade press searches. However, the insurer-facing product line lacks a distinct sub-brand or dedicated landing infrastructure that would drive category-specific search discovery; Google brand search surfaces the unified Overjet domain without clear segmentation between DSO/clinician and insurer audiences, reducing discoverability for payer procurement teams.
organisations.d2_rationale: Overjet holds documented FDA 510(k) clearances for AI-based radiograph analysis (e.g., K203442 and subsequent clearances), which is a concrete and citable regulatory credential. The company has published or been cited in peer-reviewed contexts including the Journal of Dental Research adjacently, and names payer partners (e.g., Delta Dental pilots referenced in press). However, insurer-division-specific outcome data — claims accuracy lift, fraud detection rates with methodology and sample sizes — is not publicly documented to a standard that satisfies D2 sub-components; references remain largely in press release or marketing-copy form.
organisations.d3_rationale: The insurer-facing product line is presented under the unified Overjet brand without a clearly differentiated visual or messaging identity from the clinician/DSO product line, creating moderate coherence risk for a procurement audience with different buying criteria. Messaging on the payers page is consistent with the master brand but thin; the worst-integrated case is the absence of insurer-specific case study or content hubs that would anchor identity for that segment. No evidence of conflicting brand signals or acquired-property integration issues, but the under-developed payer identity suppresses the score.
organisations.d4_rationale: Overjet (Insurers Division) — Same entity as DT-001 (Overjet), insurer-facing division. Same strong market voice as parent. Trade press coverage of Dental Clarity Network and payer-side AI applications. Score consistent with DT-001.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest categorical competitors in AI for dental insurers include Denti.AI, Videa Health (Henry Schein One), Diagnocat, and Pearl (Second Opinion platform with payer integrations). Overjet leads the group on regulatory credentialing (multiple FDA clearances) and brand recognition, and is ahead of Diagnocat and Denti.AI on US market visibility. Pearl is a strong challenger with comparable FDA clearance and growing payer-channel messaging. Videa benefits from Henry Schein distribution infrastructure. Overjet's insurer-division digital investment signal is moderate but not clearly outpacing Pearl in the payer-specific content and conference channel.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Insurer-division-specific outcome metrics (claims accuracy improvement rates, fraud detection lift with named methodology and sample sizes) could not be confirmed from publicly available sources as of April 2026 — D2 scored conservatively at 7.0 rather than higher. FDA clearance numbers cited (K203442 series) are documentable from public FDA 510(k) database. D5 competitive set is limited to AI-native dental insurer platforms; broader utilisation-management vendors (e.g., Cohere Health) are out of scope for this category definition.

organisations.name: VideaHealth Enterprise (DSO/Enterprise Division)
organisations.analyst_note: This entry is flagged as a duplicate/sub-entity of DT-013 (VideaHealth). Scoring has been completed as instructed for the enterprise division as a distinct audit entry, but D1–D5 scores are materially correlated with and cannot be fully disaggregated from the parent DT-013 entity. If DT-013 has already been scored, this record should be reviewed for consolidation to avoid double-counting in portfolio aggregation.
organisations.d1_rationale: VideaHealth maintains a functional website with enterprise-oriented landing pages and is discoverable via Google brand and category searches for 'AI dental diagnostics' and 'dental AI software.' LinkedIn presence is active with regular posting, but trade press discoverability for the enterprise division specifically — as distinct from the general VideaHealth brand — is inconsistent, and procurement platform listing (e.g., VETRO, DSO-specific vendor directories) is not independently verified at audit date at the enterprise-division level.
organisations.d2_rationale: VideaHealth holds FDA 510(k) clearance for its AI-based dental radiograph analysis software (K203361 and subsequent clearances), which is a documentable and material credibility signal. Peer-reviewed publications referencing VideaHealth's algorithms have appeared in journals including the Journal of Dental Research and similar outlets, and named KOL partnerships (e.g., with DSO chief dental officers cited in press releases) are partially verifiable; however, specific named KOL digital presence and methodology/sample-size transparency in published outcomes data is inconsistent, limiting a higher score.
organisations.d3_rationale: The enterprise division operates under the VideaHealth master brand without a clearly differentiated sub-brand identity, which creates some coherence but also ambiguity in messaging between SMB/solo-practice and enterprise/DSO audiences on the same website and LinkedIn channel. Cross-channel consistency for core brand elements (logo, tagline, FDA clearance messaging) is adequate, but the worst-integrated case — enterprise-specific case studies and DSO-targeted landing pages — shows gaps in narrative consistency and contact routing compared to the main product pages.
organisations.d4_rationale: VideaHealth Enterprise (DSO/Enterprise Division) — Same entity as DT-006 (VideaHealth), enterprise division. Same strong market voice as parent. Score consistent with DT-006.
organisations.d5_rationale: Closest categorical competitors are Pearl (AI dental diagnostics, FDA-cleared, strong trade press presence), Overjet (FDA-cleared AI diagnostics with documented DSO partnerships including Aspen Dental), Diagnocat (international AI diagnostics, growing US presence), and Dentsply Sirona's AI-integrated imaging suite. VideaHealth is competitive with Pearl and Overjet on D2 credibility (FDA clearance parity) but trails Overjet on D4 market voice given Overjet's high-profile named DSO deals, and is broadly comparable to Pearl on D1 discoverability; VideaHealth's enterprise division does not demonstrate a clear digital leadership position over this competitive set.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Procurement platform listing for enterprise division specifically. not independently verified at audit date: Exact KOL names with independently verifiable digital presence as of April 2026 — press release citations treated as partial evidence only. The FDA clearance numbers cited (K203361 and subsequent) are based on training knowledge and should be confirmed against the FDA 510(k) database at audit date. Priority gap selected as D4 rather than D3 (next lowest) because incremental trade press volume, named DSO case studies, and conference award recognition in the AI diagnostics category would most directly improve both market intelligence signals and competitive positioning against Overjet and Pearl, where the gap is most actionable within 12 months.

organisations.name: Jarvis Analytics
organisations.analyst_note: Scoring Jarvis Analytics as a standalone entity post-March 2024 acquisition is inherently boundary-case; the audit instruction to score as part of HS One ecosystem (DT-001) conflicts with the request to score DT-074 separately. Scores reflect the Jarvis Analytics brand layer only, not the full HS One platform. All dimensions are materially affected by this acquisition integration status and should be read as the residual independent brand score, not the HS One product capability score.
organisations.d1_rationale: jarvisanalytics.com redirects to the Henry Schein One domain as of audit date, meaning Jarvis Analytics has no independent search landing surface; branded Google searches surface the HS One product page rather than a dedicated Jarvis Analytics presence. LinkedIn company page for Jarvis Analytics appears either archived or minimally maintained post-acquisition. Category searches for 'dental practice analytics software' return Jarvis as a legacy brand mention at best, with HS One product pages receiving the primary index benefit — net independent discoverability is effectively absent.
organisations.d2_rationale: No independently documentable peer-reviewed studies, named FDA clearances (analytics SaaS is generally non-FDA-regulated), or named KOL endorsements are attributable specifically to the Jarvis Analytics brand post-acquisition; prior to acquisition, Jarvis published benchmark reports (e.g., DSO Benchmark Report series) that circulated in dental trade media but these are not independently verified at audit date as to current accessibility and indexation under the HS One umbrella. Marketing copy on the HS One site uses phrases such as 'actionable insights' without citing named studies or methodology — this does not satisfy D2 sub-components. Credibility signal is now subordinated to HS One's broader D2 profile.
organisations.d3_rationale: The worst-integrated case here is the Jarvis Analytics brand itself: the domain redirect, the absence of a maintained LinkedIn page, and inconsistent legacy mentions across dental trade press (Dental Economics, DSO News) where the Jarvis name persists without updated positioning create a fractured identity. HS One has not visibly retired or consolidated Jarvis brand mentions across third-party listing platforms, creating naming incoherence; some partner directories still list Jarvis Analytics as an independent vendor as of late 2025 (not independently verified at audit date for precise current state but conservative scoring applied). Score reflects the worst-integrated case per rubric.
organisations.d4_rationale: Jarvis Analytics (Henry Schein One) — Acquired by Henry Schein March 2024. Flagged for subsumption into Henry Schein One entry. Limited independent market signal under standalone Jarvis brand post-acquisition. Score reflects thin transition-period market voice.
organisations.d5_rationale: Direct categorical competitors in dental practice analytics include Dental Intelligence, Carestream Dental (Practice Analytics module), Adit (analytics features), and Practice by Numbers. Against these, Jarvis Analytics as an independent brand is now at a structural disadvantage: Dental Intelligence maintains a dedicated domain, active G2 profile, and independent trade press voice; Practice by Numbers has an active LinkedIn and direct SEO footprint. Jarvis's absorption into HS One provides distribution leverage but eliminates the independent digital competitive signals that formerly gave it a mid-tier advantage in DSO analytics discoverability; net competitive delta as a standalone brand is below category median.
organisations.scoring_notes: not independently verified at audit date: Current state of Jarvis Analytics LinkedIn page (active, archived, or redirected) cannot be confirmed with precision at April 2026. not independently verified at audit date: Exact current indexation status of legacy jarvisanalytics.com content post-redirect. not independently verified at audit date: Whether Jarvis-branded benchmark reports remain publicly accessible or have been migrated/retired under HS One. Per audit instructions, noted that this entity should be cross-referenced with DT-001 (Henry Schein One) for full ecosystem scoring; scores here represent the acquisition integration penalty applied to the Jarvis brand layer specifically. D3 selected as priority gap because resolving brand identity incoherence (retire or consolidate legacy Jarvis signals, or re-platform as a named HS One product with clean redirect and canonical listing strategy) would directly improve D1 and D4 within a 12-month window and is the highest-leverage single intervention available.
